nodes.description: Isocyanide (carbylamine) R-NC — foul-smelling compound; product of carbylamine reaction; distinguishing test for primary amines

reagents: (1) LiAlH4 (lithium aluminium hydride) in dry ether
edges.conditions: LiAlH₄; dry ether; 0°C; anhydrous; then H₂O workup. Acid chloride is most reactive toward LiAlH₄ (even NaBH₄ reduces acid chlorides). RCOCl → RCHO (aldehyde, which is immediately reduced further by excess LiAlH₄) → RCH₂OH (primary alcohol). Alternatively: 2 equiv NaBH₄ in EtOH reduces acid chloride → alcohol. Rosenmund: DIBALH or H₂/Pd-BaSO₄ stops at aldehyde.
edges.notes: LAH reduction of acyl halide → primary alcohol: acetyl chloride (ethanoyl chloride, CH₃COCl) + (1)LiAlH₄ → (2)H₂O → ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH); GENERAL: R-C(=O)-Cl + LiAlH₄ → R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol); MECHANISM: H⁻ attacks the C of acyl chloride C=O → Cl⁻ leaves (acyl chlorides are the MOST REACTIVE acid derivative; Cl⁻ is an excellent leaving group) → R-CHO (aldehyde) intermediate forms → LAH immediately reduces R-CHO → R-CH₂-OH; COMPARISON: acyl chloride + LAH is very fast (most reactive acid derivative); no isolation of intermediate possible; CONTRAST: Rosenmund reduction (H₂/Pd-BaSO₄ poisoned) selectively reduces R-COCl → R-CHO (aldehyde; stops there; the BaSO₄ poisoning of Pd slows over-reduction); LAH goes all the way to R-CH₂-OH
edges.exception_description: Rosenmund_stops_at_CHO_not_CH2OH → Exception to the standard rule: Rosenmund stops at cho not ch2oh. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | DIBALH_over_reduces_ester_to_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Dibalh over reduces ester to alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Curtius rearrangement: Step 1 (acyl azide formation): RT; Et₂O or THF; NaN₃ + RCOCl → RCON₃ + NaCl. Step 2 (rearrangement): gentle heat (50–80°C) → RCON₃ loses N₂ → isocyanate R-N=C=O. Step 3: H₂O → carbamic acid → CO₂ + RNH₂. Or trap isocyanate with ROH → urethane (carbamate). One C fewer than acid chloride. Milder than Schmidt.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: Kolbe_Schmidt_gives_para_product_always → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe schmidt gives para product always. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid); RT; anhydrous CH₂Cl₂ or CS₂. R''COCl + AlCl₃ → acylium (R''CO⁺) → attacks ether O → cleaves one C-O bond → ester + R'X. Friedel-Crafts-like mechanism on ether. Anhydrous conditions required.

reagent_1.conditions: 2 EtOH + COCl₂ → EtOCOOEt + 2 HCl; base (Et₃N or pyridine) to neutralise HCl; anhydrous; RT. Phosgene reacts with two alcohols sequentially → diethyl carbonate (carbonate ester). Highly toxic — handle in fume hood. Alternatively use dimethyl carbonate (DMC) as safer phosgene equivalent.
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. edges.conditions: Gilman reagent (dimethylcuprate, Me₂CuLi); dry ether; –78°C to 0°C; anhydrous. Me₂CuLi + CH₃COCl → CH₃COCH₃ (acetone). Organocuprate adds to acyl halide (1,2-addition, ketone product; no over-addition to ketone). Mechanism: transmetalation → reductive elimination. Advantage over Grignard: Grignard + RCOCl → 3° alcohol (adds twice); cuprate stops at ketone.
edges.notes: Gilman reagent + acyl chloride → ketone: acetyl chloride (ethanoyl chloride, CH₃-C(=O)-Cl) + dimethylcuprate lithium (Me₂CuLi) → propan-2-one (acetone, CH₃-C(=O)-CH₃) + LiCl + CuX; GENERAL: R'-C(=O)-Cl + R₂CuLi → R'-C(=O)-R (ketone) + LiCl + CuX; this is one of the BEST methods to make KETONES via C-C bond formation; SELECTIVITY: Gilman (R₂CuLi) reacts with acyl chlorides → KETONE; UNLIKE GRIGNARD: Grignard with acyl chloride → gives over-addition (Grignard adds twice → tertiary alcohol, not ketone); Gilman stops at ketone (less reactive → does NOT add to the ketone product); MECHANISM: R₂CuLi acts as a 'soft' nucleophile → oxidative addition/transmetalation pathway (not direct SN2); the acyl chloride C=O is attacked → tetrahedral intermediate → Cl⁻ eliminated → ketone; only ONE R from R₂CuLi is transferred; SCOPE: can use complex RLi → R₂CuLi → react with R'-COCl → R'-CO-R (mixed ketone with 2 different R groups)
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Expecting mono-addition with esters

edges.conditions: RT; anhydrous CH₂Cl₂ solvent; PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) or PDC. Mild oxidant: stops at aldehyde from primary alcohol. Does NOT oxidise to carboxylic acid. Secondary alcohol → ketone. Tertiary alcohol → no reaction. Alternative mild oxidants: Swern (oxalyl chloride/DMSO/Et₃N, –78°C), MnO₂ (allylic/benzylic only), Dess-Martin periodinane (DMP).

reagent_1.conditions: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate); CH₂Cl₂; RT; anhydrous. Selective oxidation of primary alcohol → aldehyde (stops at aldehyde, no overoxidation to acid in dry CH₂Cl₂). PCC does NOT continue to carboxylic acid. Alternatives: Swern (oxalyl chloride/DMSO/Et₃N, –78°C), Dess-Martin periodinane, MnO₂ (only
reagent_1.note: Primary alcohol → aldehyde using MILD oxidants (STOPS at aldehyde, does NOT over-oxidize to acid): PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) / PDC (pyridinium dichromate) / Collins (CrO3·2py) / Swern (oxalyl chloride+DMSO+NEt3) / Moffatt (DCC+DMSO) / Cu/Delta / X2 / NBS / MnO2 / CAN; propan-1-ol + PCC → propanal; KEY: these mild oxidants are used when you want primary alcohol → aldehyde selectively without over-oxidation; CONTRAST: strong oxidants (KMnO4/H+, K2Cr2O7/H+) over-oxidize primary alcohol → carboxylic acid (NOT useful for aldehyde synthesis); secondary alcohol + same mild oxidants → ketone (ketones are stable to over-oxidation); BEST for secondary: CrO3/pyridinium (circled in notes)

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Primary alcohol → aldehyde using MILD oxidants (STOPS at aldehyde, does NOT over-oxidize to acid): PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) / PDC (pyridinium dichromate) / Collins (CrO3·2py) / Swern (oxalyl chloride+DMSO+NEt3) / Moffatt (DCC+DMSO) / Cu/Delta / X2 / NBS / MnO2 / CAN; propan-1-ol + PCC → propanal; KEY: these mild oxidants are used when you want primary alcohol → aldehyde selectively without over-oxidation; CONTRAST: strong oxidants (KMnO4/H+, K2Cr2O7/H+) over-oxidize primary alcohol → carboxylic acid (NOT useful for aldehyde synthesis); secondary alcohol + same mild oxidants → ketone (ketones are stable to over-oxidation); BEST for secondary: CrO3/pyridinium (circled in notes)

genuine_levers: PCC stops at aldehyde (primary alcohol); KMnO4 over-oxidises to carboxylic acid — oxidant choice is a genuine lever
common_traps: PCC does NOT oxidise secondary alcohols to carboxylic acids (stops at ketone for 2°, aldehyde for 1°)
Tertiary alcohols are NOT oxidised by PCC — if starting material is tertiary, this step gives no reaction
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (oxidant selectivity: PCC vs KMnO4 vs CrO3)

edges.exception_description: Aldehydes oxidised by KMnO₄ → PCC oxidises 1° alcohol to aldehyde and stops there; KMnO₄ goes all the way to carboxylic acid | Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | Oxidation of primary amine RCH2NH2 with KMnO4 gives RNO2 → RCH2NH2 + KMnO4 → RCHO → RCOOH (over-oxidation path); tertiary primary amine R3CNH2 gives R3C-NO2 | KMnO4_H_plus_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_aldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: Kmno4 h plus oxidizes primary alcohol to aldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | tert_alkylbenzene_oxidized_by_KMnO4_to_benzoic_acid → Exception to the standard rule: Tert alkylbenzene oxidized by kmno4 to benzoic acid. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | PCC_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_carboxylic_acid_like_Jones → Exception to the standard rule: Pcc oxidizes primary alcohol to carboxylic acid like jones. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Choosing wrong oxidant for target oxidation level

edges.conditions: Reflux; concentrated H₂SO₄ (>170°C, >443K) or Al₂O₃ (>300°C) or POCl₃/pyridine; E1 or E2 mechanism. Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) preferred. H₂SO₄ mechanism: OH protonated → oxonium → loss of H₂O → carbocation → E1 → alkene. Order of ease: 3° > 2° > 1°. Rearrangement possible if more stable carbocation accessible via 1,2-shift.

reagent_1.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; >170°C (>443K); E1 mechanism. Mechanism: H⁺ protonates OH → oxonium → loss of H₂O → carbocation → loss of β-H → alkene. Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene). Rearrangements possible. Order of ease: 3°ROH > 2°ROH >> 1°ROH. Temperature critical: >170°C for alkene (vs 110–170°C for et
reagent_1.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_2.conditions: Al₂O₃ (alumina); >300°C; gas-phase or solid-liquid contact; E2 mechanism (base-like behaviour of Al₂O₃ surface). Saytzeff alkene. No rearrangements (no carbocation). Milder than H₂SO₄ at same T. Used for primary alcohols that would require very high T with H₂SO₄. Industrial: ethylene from ethanol/Al
reagent_2.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_3.conditions: POCl₃ + pyridine; 0°C to RT (or mild heat); anhydrous. Mechanism: POCl₂ ester formed from ROH → pyridine (base) deprotonates β-H → E2 → alkene (Saytzeff). No rearrangements (concerted E2). Mild conditions (no carbocation). Excellent for sensitive substrates. Pyridine serves as both base and solvent.
reagent_3.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_4.conditions: KHSO₄ (potassium bisulphate); Δ (100–200°C); melt or solution; mild acid dehydration. E1 mechanism (acid-catalysed): KHSO₄ protonates OH → oxonium → loss of H₂O → carbocation → E1 → Saytzeff. Milder than H₂SO₄ (lower acid concentration). Gives Saytzeff product.
reagent_4.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_5.conditions: ZnCl₂ (Lewis acid); Δ; neat or in HCl. ZnCl₂ coordinates to OH (Lewis acid-base) → activated leaving group → elimination. E1-like. Less common dehydration agent. Also used in Lucas test (ZnCl₂/conc HCl): tests for 1°/2°/3° alcohols by speed of turbidity development.
reagent_5.note: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.

reagent_6.conditions: E1 = unimolecular elimination via carbocation. E2 = bimolecular, concerted anti-periplanar β-H removal. Zaitsev = more substituted alkene (major). Hofmann = less substituted alkene (major). ThO₂ is unique: gives Hofmann product via E1 pathway.
reagent_6.note: All dehydrating agents give Zaitsev EXCEPT ThO₂ (thorium dioxide) which gives Hofmann (less substituted/anti-Zaitsev). POCl₃ and Al₂O₃ both give E2. KHSO₄ and H₂SO₄ give E1. H₃PO₄ gives E1. Critical exam point: ThO₂ = only dehydration agent giving anti-Zaitsev (Hofmann-like) product. Do NOT confuse with E2 mechanisms which always give Zaitsev here.

edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product. | All dehydrating agents give Zaitsev EXCEPT ThO₂ (thorium dioxide) which gives Hofmann (less substituted/anti-Zaitsev). POCl₃ and Al₂O₃ both give E2. KHSO₄ and H₂SO₄ give E1. H₃PO₄ gives E1. Critical exam point: ThO₂ = only dehydration agent giving anti-Zaitsev (Hofmann-like) product. Do NOT confuse with E2 mechanisms which always give Zaitsev here.
edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Many students apply Saytzeff universally

edges.conditions: RT; anhydrous conditions; SOCl₂ with pyridine (→ R-Cl, SN2, inversion) or PBr₃ (→ R-Br, inversion) or HX (R-Cl, R-Br, R-I; SN1 for 3°, SN2 for 1°). SOCl₂ + pyridine: pyridine prevents SNi (retention), ensures SN2 (inversion). SOCl₂ without pyridine (SNi, retention of configuration). PCl₅: vigorous conditions, inversion. Lucas test: ZnCl₂/conc HCl — 3° (immediate turbidity), 2° (turbidity after heating), 1° (no reaction cold).

reagent_1.conditions: PCl₅ (phosphorus pentachloride); anhydrous; RT or mild heat; no solvent or dry CCl₄. Mechanism: ROH + PCl₅ → R-O-PCl₄ (chlorophosphorane intermediate) → SN2 by Cl⁻ on C → R-Cl + POCl₃ (phosphoryl chloride) + HCl. Inversion of configuration (SN2 mechanism). Primary and secondary alcohols react well;
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: SOCl₂ + pyridine; anhydrous; RT. Pyridine: (1) neutralises HCl; (2) forms acylpyridinium chloride → Cl⁻ attacks C backside (intermolecular SN2) → inversion. Without pyridine: SNi (retention). With pyridine: SN2 (inversion). Both routes give R-Cl from R-OH but with opposite stereochemistry.
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions: Primary alcohols (R-CH₂-OH) react with HX via SN2: acid protonates OH → R-CH₂-OH₂⁺ (water becomes the leaving group, pKa ~−1.7 for H₃O⁺ LG, excellent LG). Then X⁻ (from HX) attacks from the back face (SN2) → R-CH₂-X + H₂O. UNREARRANGED product only (SN2 = no C⁺ intermediate = no rearrangement). Rate
reagent_3.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_4.conditions: 2° and 3° alcohols react with HX via SN1: protonation of OH → oxonium → -H₂O → carbocation (C⁺) → X⁻ captures from both faces → racemic R-X (or rearranged if C⁺ undergoes 1,2-shift). 3° faster than 2° (more stable C⁺). Rate: HI > HBr > HCl >> HF (same trend as 1°). HF excluded (weak acid + F⁻ poor N
reagent_4.note: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

reagent_5.conditions: Lucas Test: HCl + anhydrous ZnCl₂. ZnCl₂ is a Lewis acid that coordinates with OH of alcohol → activates C-O bond (better LG) → SN1 for 2°/3° or SN2 (with heat) for 1°. Product R-Cl is insoluble in HCl/ZnCl₂ medium → white turbidity. Diagnostic: INSTANT turbidity = 3° (fast SN1); 5-10 min turbidity
reagent_5.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

genuine_levers: Primary/secondary: SN2 with inversion (pyridine required for retention-free SN2); tertiary: elimination competes strongly — Lucas reagent (conc. HCl/ZnCl2) is the textbook route for tertiary
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: inherits from alcohol; pyridine forces inversion at existing stereocenter (SN2); do NOT combine pyridine with SN1 claims
common_traps: pyridine + SOCl2 = SN2/inversion; claiming SN1 in the same step is mechanistically incoherent
Tertiary alcohols + SOCl2 prefer elimination, not substitution — use HCl/ZnCl2 for clean tertiary chloride
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (mechanism discrimination: SN1 vs SN2 vs E2 based on substrate class and base)

edges.exception_description: Lucas test: 3° alcohol reacts immediately → 1° aliphatic alcohols give no turbidity at room temperature; only react on heating. Allyl/benzyl alcohols (1°) react rapidly due to carbocation stability | Cyclopropylcarbinol (cyclopropyl-CH₂-OH, a primary alcohol) gives slow or no turbidity in Lucas test (no turbidity at RT, turbidity only on heating) → Cyclopropylcarbinol gives INSTANT turbidity in the Lucas test, behaving like a TERTIARY alcohol. Despite being a primary alcohol (OH on a CH₂ group), it undergoes fast SN1 because the CYCLOPROPYLCARBINYL CATION is unusually stable. The cyclopropyl ring orbitals (Walsh orbitals of the cyclopropane ring, which have partial π character) can overlap with the adjacent empty p orbital of the carbocation → stabilization equivalent to or greater than a secondary carbocation. Rate of ionization: cyclopropylcarbinyl > allylcarbinyl > cyclobutyl > cyclopentyl (in solvolysis). So Lucas test gives instant turbidity for cyclopropylcarbinol even though it's formally primary. Similarly, cyclopropanol (secondary) also gives instant turbidity (the ring opens to give allyl/homoallyl cation upon ionization). | Any secondary alcohol gives turbidity in 5-10 minutes in the Lucas test, as a reliable indicator of 2° classification → Secondary alcohols with EWG groups (especially CN or C=O) adjacent to the C-OH give NO turbidity in the Lucas test even after 10 minutes — behaving like primary alcohols. Example: an α-cyano secondary alcohol (NC-C(OH)- with another CN on the same C or adjacent C) → No turbidity (circled in the notes). The reason: CN has a strong −M effect (withdrawal) that DESTABILIZES the adjacent C⁺. When ZnCl₂/HCl tries to generate the 2° C⁺ via SN1, the CN group withdraws electron density → C⁺ is too unstable to form → no SN1 → no R-Cl → no turbidity. The −M effect of CN (and carbonyl C=O) at α-position blocks SN1 even for 2° substrates. This is the same reason α-haloketones don't do SN1 (per RM lec3 notes). | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test.
edges.exception_trap: Forgetting that benzylic/allylic 1° alcohols behave like 3° in Lucas test

edges.conditions: 450°C; Al₂O₃ catalyst; gas phase; high temperature. Mechanism: dehydration of alcohol → alkene (or ether) → then amine formation via Leuckart mechanism or direct substitution. Al₂O₃ catalyses both dehydration and amine synthesis. Preferentially gives tertiary amine (thermodynamic product at high T). Industrial route to triethylamine from ethanol + NH₃.

reagent_1.conditions: Ritter reaction: conc H₂SO₄ (or other strong acid) + HCN (or nitrile R'CN); RT to mild heat. Mechanism: acid protonates alcohol → carbocation → HCN (nucleophile, via N) attacks → nitrilium ion (R-C≡N-H⁺ or R-N⁺=C:) → H₂O addition → amide → hydrolysis → amine. 3° alcohols react best (most stable carb
reagent_1.note: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

edges.conditions: CH₂N₂ (diazomethane); RT; anhydrous ether solvent; rapid O-methylation. Mechanism: proton abstraction from R-OH by CH₂N₂ → methyl diazonium → SN2 by RO⁻ → R-OCH₃ + N₂. Selectivity of CH₂N₂: RCOOH >> ArOH > ROH (pKa order). Phenol methyl ethers easy (rate: PhOH reacts at RT; ROH requires slight warming or longer time). Hazard: CH₂N₂ is toxic and explosive gas.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.

edges.conditions: Reflux; acidic aqueous conditions; KMnO₄/H⁺ or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ or Jones reagent. Primary alcohol → aldehyde (intermediate) → carboxylic acid (overoxidation). Aqueous conditions allow the aldehyde hydrate to form, which is then oxidised. Use Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone) for clean conversion. Secondary alcohol → ketone (NOT further oxidised to acid under these conditions).

reagents: Jones reagent (CrO3 + H2SO4 + H2O in acetone solvent)
reagent_2.conditions: Jones reagent (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone); or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄/heat; or KMnO₄/H⁺. Primary alcohol → carboxylic acid (via aldehyde intermediate, which is oxidised further in aqueous acidic conditions). Jones is aggressive → aldehyde → acid. PCC stops at aldehyde. Aqueous Cr(VI) or KMnO₄: 1° ROH → RCOOH.
reagent_2.note: Strong oxidant oxidation of primary alcohol → carboxylic acid: propan-1-ol (1-propanol, RCH₂OH) + Jones reagent → propanoic acid (RCOOH); JONES REAGENT: CrO₃ + H₂SO₄ + H₂O + acetone (solvent); active species = H₂CrO₄ (chromic acid); MECHANISM: R-CH₂-OH + HO-Cr(=O)-OH (H₂CrO₄) → ester intermediate R-CH₂-O-Cr(=O)-OH → β-H elimination → R-CHO (aldehyde intermediate) → the aldehyde adds H₂O (in aqueous Jones) → R-CH(OH)₂ (gem-diol) → gem-diol + H₂CrO₄ → second oxidation → RCOOH; KEY: Jones goes 1° → RCOOH (all the way; NOT selective like PCC); 2° → ketone (stops at ketone; no further oxidation of ketone under Jones); 3° → NO reaction; OTHER STRONG OXIDANTS (same 1°→RCOOH): K₂Cr₂O₇/H+, KMnO₄/H+, HNO₃, H₂CrO₄, Mn⁷+ aq, Cr⁶+ aq medium; DIFFERENCE FROM PCC: PCC gives 1°→CHO (anhydrous, no water; cannot form gem-diol intermediate); Jones gives 1°→RCOOH (aqueous; gem-diol forms → second oxidation)

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; anhydrous conditions; base (pyridine or Et₃N) to neutralise HCl generated; acyl chloride (RCOCl) + alcohol → ester + HCl. Alternatively acyl anhydride (slow, no base needed). Fischer esterification: RCOOH + R'OH, H⁺ cat., reflux, equilibrium (remove H₂O). Acyl chloride route: fast, irreversible, suitable for sterically hindered or acid-sensitive alcohols.

reagent_1.conditions: Acetic anhydride (Ac₂O) or acetyl chloride (AcCl); RT to mild heat (Ac₂O) or 0°C (AcCl); base (pyridine or Et₃N) to neutralise HCl from AcCl. Each OH group adds 42 Da (CH₂CO contribution = 42.04). MW increase = 42 × n(OH). Used to determine n(OH) by MW measurement. Acylation is O-selective; N-acylat

reagent_2.conditions: HONO (HNO₂, in situ from NaNO₂ + HCl); 0–5°C; aqueous-alcoholic. Mechanism: N-protonated HNO₂ → NO⁺ (nitrosonium) → O-attack by alcohol → alkyl nitrite R-ONO. Primary and secondary alcohols. Alkyl nitrites are esters of nitrous acid (HO-N=O); volatile, fragrant, vasodilating (amyl nitrite = 'poppers
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions: Mechanism: acidic O-H protonates CH₂N₂ → methyl diazonium (CH₃-N₂⁺) → SN2 on methyl → R-O⁻ attacks CH₃ and N₂ leaves → R-OCH₃. Reactivity: R-COOH > Ph-OH > R-OH (matches acidity/pKa order; more acidic = better O-H proton donor for CH₂N₂ activation).
reagent_3.note: Key: CH₂N₂ reacts with O-H bonds (not C=O of aldehyde/ketone directly). Selective: if compound has COOH and OH, 1 eq. CH₂N₂ methylates COOH first (most acidic). PhOH + CH₂N₂ → anisole (methoxybenzene). Aldehyde does NOT undergo simple O-methylation with CH₂N₂. CH₂N₂ + alkene (CH₂=CH₂) → [1,3-dipolar cycloaddition] → pyrazoline → loss of N₂ → cyclopropane. CH₂N₂ + alkyne (HC≡CH) → pyrazole (6π aromatic, major product).

Alternatively: conc H₂SO₄ at 110–140°C for symmetrical ether (inter-molecular dehydration, SN2). Tertiary alcohols → only alkene under acid (E1 preferred).

Requires 1° or 2° alcohol (3° → alkene). Temperature critical: 110–140°C → ether; >170°C → alkene. Also: Al₂O₃/250°C → ether; Williamson with Na gives
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; 110–140°C (110°C < T < 170°C); SN2 bimolecular mechanism (not E1 — too cool for elimination). Oxonium ion formation → 2nd ROH attacks C → ether + H₂O (SN2). Works best for 1° alcohols (2° may give mixed results; 3° → only alkene). Industrial production of diethyl ether uses 140°C.

Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone): secondary alcohol → ketone. Swern oxidation (–78°C): secondary → ketone. Oppenauer oxidation (Al-alkoxide/ketone, mild): secondary → ketone.

reagent_1.conditions: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) in CH₂Cl₂; RT or mild Swern (oxalyl chloride/DMSO/Et₃N, –78°C); or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ (Jones, in acetone). 2° alcohol → ketone. PCC: mild, anhydrous, selective. Jones: aqueous, orange CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone. Oppenauer (Al-t-BuO/cyclohexanone, RT): 2° alcohol → ketone. All stop
reagent_1.note: Secondary alcohol → ketone: propan-2-ol (isopropanol, 2° alcohol) + PCC (or PDC, Collins, Corey, Swern, Moffatt, Jones, X₂, NBS, Cu/300°C) → propan-2-one (acetone, ketone); GENERAL: any 2° alcohol + any oxidant from the selective list → ketone; ALL oxidants (both selective and strong) oxidize 2° alcohol → ketone; KETONE CANNOT BE FURTHER OXIDIZED under normal conditions (no α-H? then completely resistant; even strong KMnO₄/H+ gives no rxn with 2° ketone product under normal conditions); under VERY HIGH TEMPERATURE and STRONG OA (hot KMnO₄, HNO₃, K₂Cr₂O₇/Δ): ketone undergoes Popoff's Rule cleavage → two carboxylic acids (C-C bonds alpha to C=O are cleaved; RCOOH formed from each fragment); TERTIARY (3°) → always NO REACTION under all standard selective oxidants

edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; <110°C (<383K); direct esterification of OH by H₂SO₄. Product: alkyl hydrogen sulphate (monoester of H₂SO₄, R-OSO₃H). No dehydration at this low temperature. Reversible on warming. Used industrially (e.g., ethyl sulphate from ethanol + H₂SO₄ cold). At higher T: ether (110–170°C) → alkene (>170°C).

edges.conditions: RT; dry HCl or p-TsOH (acid catalyst); excess dry alcohol (2 equiv ROH); anhydrous conditions (remove H₂O to drive equilibrium). Mechanism: H⁺ protonates C=O → oxocarbenium → first ROH attack → hemiacetal → protonation of OH → loss of H₂O → oxocarbenium → 2nd ROH → acetal. Reverse (hydrolysis) occurs in aqueous acid. Acetals are stable to base; used as protecting groups.
edges.notes: Protecting group strategy: acetal stable to base/LiAlH₄; removed by dilute acid. Ensure the protecting group is compatible with all subsequent steps.

edges.conditions: RT; protic solvent (EtOH or EtOH/H₂O); NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride) — mild, safe, selective. Alternative: LiAlH₄ (dry ether, anhydrous, more powerful). NaBH₄: reduces aldehydes and ketones but NOT esters, acids, amides. LiAlH₄: reduces all C=O including esters, acids, amides. Mechanism: H⁻ delivered from B or Al to electrophilic carbonyl C → alkoxide → protonation during workup.

reagent_1.conditions: 0–5°C; dilute NaOH (5–10% aq.); cold conditions (aldol condensation: cold=aldol addition product; hot=dehydrated enone). Mechanism: OH⁻ deprotonates α-H → enolate → attacks C=O of 2nd aldehyde → β-hydroxy aldehyde (aldol). Cold conditions stop at addition; heat → dehydration → α,β-unsaturated aldehy
reagent_1.note: Aldol addition (cold) vs condensation (warm): specify temperature. Crossed aldol requires careful substrate choice to avoid four-product mixture.

reagent_2.conditions: NaBH₄; EtOH or EtOH/H₂O; RT. Mild selective reduction: aldehyde → primary alcohol. NaBH₄ selective (reduces C=O not C=C). H⁻ delivers to aldehyde C → primary alcohol. Very mild: NaBH₄ + EtOH, RT, 30 min → full conversion. Workup: dilute acid or water. Alternatively: LiAlH₄/dry ether (more reactive).
reagent_2.note: LAH (or NaBH₄) reduction of aldehyde → primary alcohol: propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) + LiAlH₄ → propan-1-ol (CH₃CH₂CH₂OH); GENERAL: R-CHO + LiAlH₄ → R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol, 1°); ALSO WORKS WITH NaBH₄ (sodium borohydride), H₂/Pt, H₂/Pd, H₂/Ni; MECHANISM: H⁻ from AlH₄⁻ attacks the electrophilic C of C=O (aldehyde carbon is more electrophilic than ketone due to less steric/electronic donation from R) → alkoxide intermediate R-CH₂-O⁻ (coordinated to Al) → H₂O workup → R-CH₂-OH; ALDEHYDE REDUCTION PRODUCTS: always primary alcohol (1°); KETONE REDUCTION: always secondary alcohol (2°); SELECTIVITY: LiAlH₄ reduces ALL carbonyls (aldehyde, ketone, ester, COOH, CONH₂, C≡N, C=N); NaBH₄ is SELECTIVE (reduces aldehyde and ketone ONLY; does NOT reduce ester, COOH, amide, nitrile — too mild for these)

reagent_3.conditions: anhydrous ether solvent; addition of R⁻ (carbanion character) to C=O of HCHO → R-CH₂-OMgX → H₃O⁺ → R-CH₂-OH; adds exactly 1 carbon
reagent_3.note: Grignard + different carbonyls: HCHO → 1° alcohol (+1C); RCHO → 2° alcohol (+1C at α); R₂C=O → 3° alcohol (+1C at α); RMgX + epoxide → 1° alcohol (+2C via ring-opening at less hindered C. RMgX + O₂ → R-OOMgX → H₂O → R-OH (peroxide intermediate → alcohol). Use Grignard to homologate: each addition increases carbon count.

RMgX + O₂ → R-OOMgX → H₂O → R-OH (peroxide intermediate → alcohol). Use Grignard to homologate: each addition increases carbon count.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | Aldol condensation requires base → Acid-catalysed aldol is also possible; intramolecular aldol (Dieckmann equivalent) can cyclise to form rings under both acid and base conditions | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_requires_only_1_alpha_H → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation requires only 1 alpha h. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aldol_condensation_E2_mechanism → Exception to the standard rule: Aldol condensation e2 mechanism. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Robinson_annulation_aldol_first_then_Michael → Exception to the standard rule: Robinson annulation aldol first then michael. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure

edges.conditions: Dilute NaOH (5–10%); heat (Δ, 70–80°C); aqueous ethanol. Aldol condensation: ethanal (acetaldehyde) is both enolisable donor and electrophile acceptor. Step 1: OH⁻ + CH₃CHO → ⁻CH₂CHO (enolate) → + CH₃CHO → β-hydroxyaldehyde (aldol). Step 2: heat → dehydration → (E)-but-2-enal (crotonaldehyde, trans). Cold NaOH → aldol addition (β-hydroxy aldehyde); heat → condensation (dehydrated, conjugated).

reagent_1.conditions: NaOH (aq); RT. The Me₂N group (strong EDG) makes the ring strongly electron-rich; carbonyl is push-pull activated. NaOH treatment: OH⁻ adds to C=O → tetrahedral intermediate (zwitterion with N⁺ and O⁻ or negative charge delocalised). Quinoid zwitterion form: Me₂N⁺=Ar=CH-O⁻. This is why Meisenheimer-
reagent_1.note: 4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde (p-Me₂N-C₆H₄-CHO) + NaOH → does NOT give normal Cannizzaro products; instead forms a ZWITTERION (betaine); mechanism: OH⁻ attacks C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → the dimethylamino nitrogen becomes quaternary (N⁺) and the alkoxide (O⁻) remains → ZWITTERION (Me₂N⁺=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ quinoid/betaine form); this zwitterion is stable and does not undergo the normal Cannizzaro hydride transfer; RESULT: NO normal Cannizzaro products (no alcohol + acid); instead: zwitterionic intermediate is the final/stable product under basic conditions; similar behavior: p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (OH-Ph-CHO) + NaOH → quinoid zwitterion (⁻O=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ type); ALL aryl aldehydes with strong ELECTRON-DONATING groups in para position (p-NR₂, p-OH) → zwitterion exception to Cannizzaro; compare to EWG aryl aldehydes → deformylation exception

edges.notes: Self-aldol condensation of ethanal (acetaldehyde, CH3CHO): (1) OH- removes α-H from CH3CHO → enolate (-CH2CHO); (2) Enolate attacks C=O of another CH3CHO → β-hydroxy aldehyde (3-hydroxybutanal, CH3CH(OH)CH2CHO = aldol product); (3) E1CB: OH- removes α-H from the β-hydroxy intermediate → carbanion → β-OH leaves → forms C=C; product = (E)-but-2-enal (trans-crotonaldehyde, CH3-CH=CH-CHO); E-geometry preferred (trans more stable); CONDITION: ≥2 α-H needed for aldol condensation (1 for enolate, 1 for E1CB); RATE ∝ [CH3CHO][OH-]^1 (first order in each) | 4-(Dimethylamino)benzaldehyde (p-Me₂N-C₆H₄-CHO) + NaOH → does NOT give normal Cannizzaro products; instead forms a ZWITTERION (betaine); mechanism: OH⁻ attacks C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → the dimethylamino nitrogen becomes quaternary (N⁺) and the alkoxide (O⁻) remains → ZWITTERION (Me₂N⁺=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ quinoid/betaine form); this zwitterion is stable and does not undergo the normal Cannizzaro hydride transfer; RESULT: NO normal Cannizzaro products (no alcohol + acid); instead: zwitterionic intermediate is the final/stable product under basic conditions; similar behavior: p-hydroxybenzaldehyde (OH-Ph-CHO) + NaOH → quinoid zwitterion (⁻O=C₆H₄=CH-O⁻ type); ALL aryl aldehydes with strong ELECTRON-DONATING groups in para position (p-NR₂, p-OH) → zwitterion exception to Cannizzaro; compare to EWG aryl aldehydes → deformylation exception

edges.conditions: Step 1: Reflux in acetic acid/HCl; Zn-Hg amalgam (Clemmensen reduction). Strongly acidic conditions (conc HCl). Mechanism uncertain but involves carbenoid/zinc-carbenoid intermediate on metal surface; not via alcohol intermediate. Aldehyde or ketone directly → CH₂ (methylene). Alternative: Wolff-Kishner (NH₂NH₂/KOH/EG, reflux 200°C) — for base-sensitive substrates avoid; acid-sensitive → use Clemmensen. Both go C=O → CH₂.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: Wolff_Kishner_reduces_both_C=O_and_C=C → Exception to the standard rule: Wolff kishner reduces both c=o and c=c. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Reductive amination: Step 1 (imine formation): pH 4–5, RT or mild heat, NH₄OAc or NH₃/EtOH, –H₂O. Step 2 (reduction of imine): H₂/Pd (RT, 1 atm, EtOH) or NaBH₃CN (RT, MeOH, pH 6–7). NaBH₃CN is selective: reduces iminium (not carbonyls, unlike NaBH₄). Product: primary amine from aldehyde + NH₃; secondary amine from ketone + NH₃.

reagent_1.conditions: Reductive amination: Step 1 (imine): pH 4–5, RT, RNH₂ (primary amine) + RCHO/R₂CO, –H₂O. Step 2: NaBH₃CN (pH 6, MeOH) or H₂/Pd (1 atm, EtOH). Product: secondary amine (one new N-C bond). Catalytic hydrogenation at RT; NaBH₃CN is more selective at RT. Iminium ion (protonated imine) is the actual elec
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: Step 1: Dean-Stark, reflux toluene or benzene; 2° amine (R₂NH) + R₃CO/R₃CHO → enamine (R₃NH + ketone → iminium → deprotonation at α-C → enamine). Step 2: reduce enamine (H₂/Pd or NaBH₃CN, pH 6). Product: tertiary amine. Enamines are also useful as nucleophilic enol equivalents (alkylation then hydro
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; Tollens' reagent ([Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺, alkaline) → silver mirror (RT); Fehling's (Cu²⁺/tartrate, alkaline, heat) → Cu₂O brick-red precipitate; KMnO₄/H⁺ or K₂Cr₂O₇/H⁺ (acidic, RT to reflux). Benedict's: similar to Fehling's but citrate buffer (milder). Aldehydes oxidised; ketones NOT oxidised by Tollens'/Fehling's (except α-hydroxy ketones). Formic acid gives positive Tollens' (can be oxidised to CO₂).

reagent_1.conditions: Perkin condensation: acetic anhydride (Ac₂O, 3 equiv); potassium acetate (CH₃COOK) or sodium acetate (base/catalyst); heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Mechanism: CH₃COO⁻ deprotonates Ac₂O → enolate → nucleophilic addition to PhCHO → aldol-like → acylated β-hydroxy → elimination → PhCH=CH-COOH (cinnamic acid, tr
reagent_1.note: Perkin reaction: benzaldehyde + acetic anhydride + sodium acetate/Δ → (E)-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid (cinnamic acid, PhCH=CH-COOH); PERKIN REACTION ONLY FOR AROMATIC ALDEHYDES AND KETONES (not aliphatic); mechanism: (1) CH3COO- (sodium acetate, base) removes α-H from acetic anhydride → enolate -CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3; (2) Enolate attacks PhCHO → β-hydroxy adduct Ph-CH(OH)-CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3; (3) OH is acetylated by the second acetyl group → Ph-CH(OAc)-CH2-CO-O-CO-CH3 type; (4) E1CB or heat: loss of AcOH → Ph-CH=CH-CO-O-CO-CH3 (unsaturated anhydride); (5) H3O+/Δ: hydrolysis → PhCH=CH-COOH (cinnamic acid, E geometry); with propionic anhydride (CH3CH2CO)2O + AcONa → Ph-CH=C(CH3)-COOH (alpha-methylcinnamic acid)

reagent_2.conditions: Perkin condensation: Ac₂O + CH₃COONa; heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Same as benzaldehyde case but EWG (NO₂) on ring makes aldehyde more electrophilic → faster condensation. Product: (E)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)prop-2-enoic acid (4-nitrocinnamic acid). Perkin works best for aldehydes lacking α-H (ArCHO) because self
reagent_2.note: Perkin reaction Q2: 4-nitrobenzaldehyde (O2N-Ph-CHO) + acetic anhydride (Ac2O) + sodium acetate (AcO-Na+) → H3O+/Δ → (E)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)prop-2-enoic acid (4-nitrocinnamic acid); same Perkin mechanism as benzaldehyde; note that the EWG (NO2) activates the aromatic aldehyde C=O toward nucleophilic attack by the anhydride enolate → may give SLIGHTLY FASTER rate for the addition step but overall Cannizzaro rate is DECREASED by EWG (opposite for Perkin: EWG activates aldehyde toward enolate addition); Perkin works for all aromatic aldehydes (EWG or EDG on ring); product = always E (trans) geometry (more stable, thermodynamic)

reagent_3.conditions: Perkin with propionic anhydride: (CH₃CH₂CO)₂O + CH₃CH₂COONa (sodium propanoate); heat (Δ, 150–170°C). Propionic anhydride enolate attacks PhCHO → β-hydroxy acyl → elimination → α-methylcinnamic acid. α-Methyl substituent from propionic anhydride. E-isomer (trans) thermodynamically preferred.
reagent_3.note: Perkin reaction with propionic anhydride (propanoic anhydride): PhCHO + (CH3CH2CO)2O + CH3CH2COO-Na+ → H3O+/Δ → (E)-2-methyl-3-phenylprop-2-enoic acid (alpha-methylcinnamic acid, Ph-CH=C(CH3)-COOH); the alpha-carbon of the anhydride (CH3-CH2 in propionic anhydride → the CH2 is the alpha-carbon) gets incorporated into the product; the alpha-substituent (CH3 from propionyl) ends up at the alpha-carbon of the product acid; GENERAL RULE: Perkin with (RCO)2O → Ar-CH=C(R)-COOH (R = rest of acyl group, one carbon shorter); for Ac2O (R=CH3) → one α-carbon, no substituent (R=H effectively) → Ar-CH=CH-COOH; for Pr2O (R=CH2CH3) → Ar-CH=C(CH3)-COOH

reagents: or K2Cr2O7/H+, HNO3, Jones reagent, H2CrO4, Tollens, Fehling (for aliphatic only), m-CPBA, H2O2, O3
reagent_4.conditions: KMnO₄/H⁺ or K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ (Jones) or Tollens'/Fehling's + oxidation. Aldehyde → carboxylic acid (2e⁻ oxidation). Mild oxidants: Tollens' gives silver mirror + carboxylate. Jones (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/acetone): RCHO → RCOOH cleanly. KMnO₄/acidic: RCHO → RCOOH. PCC does NOT oxidise aldehydes to acids (stops at
reagent_4.note: Aldehyde oxidation to carboxylic acid: propanal (CH₃CH₂CHO) + KMnO₄/H+ → propanoic acid (CH₃CH₂COOH); COMPLETE LIST of reagents that oxidize R-CHO → RCOOH: (1) KMnO₄/H+ (acidic, hot); (2) K₂Cr₂O₇/H+; (3) HNO₃; (4) Jones reagent (CrO₃/H₂SO₄/H₂O); (5) H₂CrO₄; (6) Mn⁷+ neutral; (7) Cr⁶+ neutral; (8) O₃ (ozonolysis); (9) H₂O₂; (10) m-CPBA (meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid); (11) Tollens (ALL aldehydes); (12) Fehling's (ALIPHATIC aldehydes only; Ar-CHO = benzaldehyde does NOT give Fehling positive); SELECTIVE REAGENTS for aldehyde (do NOT oxidize primary alcohol, only aldehyde): {O₃, H₂O₂, m-CPBA, Tollens, Fehling's} — these reagents oxidize R-CHO → RCOOH but do NOT oxidize R-CH₂-OH (primary alcohol); NON-SELECTIVE: KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, HNO₃, Jones — these oxidize BOTH primary alcohol AND aldehyde to RCOOH

edges.conditions: 0°C to RT; buffered slightly acidic or neutral conditions (pH ~5); HCN or NaCN/H₂SO₄. Mechanism: CN⁻ (nucleophile) attacks electrophilic carbonyl C → alkoxide → protonation → cyanohydrin (α-hydroxynitrile). Equilibrium (reversible). Aldehydes > ketones (less hindered). HCN alone is slow (no CN⁻); NaCN + HCl or KCN + H₂SO₄ generates CN⁻ in situ. Chiral (racemic mixture unless asymmetric catalyst used).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: LiAlH4 (LAH), dry ether, then H2O workup. Reduces aldehyde (RCHO) to primary alcohol (RCH2OH). NaBH4/EtOH is milder alternative (there is a separate edge for NaBH4). Do NOT confuse with cold KMnO4 which dihydroxylates alkenes.
edges.notes: LAH reduction: R-CHO → R-CH2-OH (primary alcohol). Stronger than NaBH4; reduces esters, acids, nitriles too. Requires anhydrous conditions (reacts with water/protic solvents).
edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | Oxidation of primary amine RCH2NH2 with KMnO4 gives RNO2 → RCH2NH2 + KMnO4 → RCHO → RCOOH (over-oxidation path); tertiary primary amine R3CNH2 gives R3C-NO2 | KMnO4_H_plus_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_aldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: Kmno4 h plus oxidizes primary alcohol to aldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | tert_alkylbenzene_oxidized_by_KMnO4_to_benzoic_acid → Exception to the standard rule: Tert alkylbenzene oxidized by kmno4 to benzoic acid. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: PCl₅; anhydrous; RT to gentle heat. R-CHO + PCl₅ → R-CHCl₂ (geminal dichloride) + POCl₃. R-CO-R' + PCl₅ → R-CCl₂-R' + POCl₃. Converts C=O to CCl₂. Product: geminal dihalide. Used to make gem-dihalides; reverse: gem-dihalide + base → alkyne or ketone (via dichlorocarbene or direct).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Slightly acidic conditions (pH 4–5); aqueous or alcoholic; –H₂O (condensation, remove water or use Dean-Stark). Mechanism: amine nucleophile attacks C=O → carbinolamine (hemiaminal) → protonation of OH → loss of H₂O → iminium → deprotonation → imine. Aldehydes > ketones (less steric). 1° amine required (2° amine → enamine). Acid catalyses dehydration; too acidic protonates amine (less nucleophilic).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Conc. NaOH (aq): Cannizzaro reaction (only no-alpha-H aldehydes, e.g. HCHO, PhCHO, (CH3)3CCHO). Disproportionation: 2 RCHO + NaOH → RCOO⁻Na⁺ + RCH2OH. One molecule oxidised to carboxylate, one reduced to alcohol.

reagents: steric crowding prevents normal nucleophilic addition
reagent_1.conditions: NaOH (aq); RT. (2,6-dimethylphenyl)methanal (2,6-dimethylbenzaldehyde) is sterically hindered → no Cannizzaro (OH⁻ cannot attack C=O easily). Instead, base causes Dakin-like or deformylation: loss of CHO group as formate → 1,3-dimethylbenzene (m-xylene). Steric crowding at C=O prevents normal nucleo
reagent_1.note: Steric crowding deformylation: (2,6-dimethylphenyl)methanal (2,6-dimethylbenzaldehyde) + NaOH → 1,3-dimethylbenzene (m-xylene) + HCOONa (sodium formate); reason = STERIC CROWDING around the CHO group; the two ortho-methyl groups (2,6-positions) flank the CHO from both sides → OH⁻ cannot attack the C=O normally (too crowded, steric hindrance prevents nucleophilic approach) → instead, the aldehyde undergoes deformylation (C-CHO bond cleavage) → Ar⁻ + HCOO⁻; NOTE: this compound does NOT give Cannizzaro reaction despite having no α-H; REASON: Cannizzaro requires OH⁻ to attack C=O (step 1), which is blocked by steric crowding; deformylation is the alternative pathway; 2,6-disubstituted benzaldehyde is specifically noted as a Cannizzaro exception 'due to crowding'

edges.notes: Cannizzaro reaction: requires no alpha-H (cannot enolise). Products are RCOOH (after acidification) + RCH2OH. Cross-Cannizzaro possible with HCHO (reduces other aldehyde, HCHO → HCOO⁻). Aldol condensation occurs instead when alpha-H is present.
