edges.conditions: HIO₄ (periodic acid); aqueous; RT. Periodate cleavage of 1,2-diol (vicinal diol): CH₃CH(OH)CH(OH)CH₃ + HIO₄ → 2 CH₃CHO (ethanal) + HIO₃ + H₂O. One equiv HIO₄ per vicinal diol. Mechanism: cyclic periodate ester → oxidative cleavage. Cleaves vicinal diols specifically (not 1,3-diols). Pb(OAc)₄ is an alternative (non-aqueous). Stoichiometric: each CHOH fragment → aldehyde/ketone/HCHO.

reagent_1.conditions: Pb(OAc)₄ (lead tetraacetate); glacial acetic acid; RT. Periodate-equivalent: Pb(OAc)₄ cleaves vicinal diols → two carbonyls. Propane-1,2-diol (1,2-propanediol) + Pb(OAc)₄ → HCHO (formaldehyde, from primary CH₂OH) + CH₃CHO (ethanal, from secondary CHOH). One equiv Pb(OAc)₄ per diol. Non-aqueous alter
reagent_1.note: Pb(OAc)₄ (lead tetraacetate) oxidative cleavage of vicinal diol: propane-1,2-diol (CH₃-CH(OH)-CH₂OH, 1,2-diol) + Pb(OAc)₄ → ethanal (CH₃CHO) + formaldehyde (HCHO) + Pb(OAc)₂ + 2 AcOH; GENERAL: 1,2-diol (vicinal diol) + Pb(OAc)₄ → two carbonyl compounds (one from each C of the former C-C-diol) + Pb(OAc)₂ + 2 AcOH; PRODUCT RULE: each C of the diol → C=O (aldehyde if that C had H; ketone if no H); if R-CH(OH)-CH(OH)-R' → R-CHO + R'-CHO; if R₂C(OH)-C(OH)R₂ → R₂C=O + R₂C=O (two ketones); MECHANISM: BOTH OH groups of the 1,2-diol simultaneously esterify with Pb(OAc)₄ → cyclic 5-membered Pb diester intermediate (Pb coordinated to both O) → C-C bond homolysis/heterolysis → two C=O + Pb(OAc)₂ (Pb reduced from +4 to +2); STEREOCHEMICAL REQUIREMENT: SYN (cis) diols form the cyclic 5-membered TS more readily; ANTI diols on rigid ring systems (trans-cyclohexane-1,2-diol where both OH are axial/trans) CANNOT form the cyclic Pb ester → do NOT react via the cyclic mechanism → lower reactivity; COMPARISON WITH HIO₄ (periodic acid): HIO₄ also cleaves 1,2-diols via a cyclic mechanism; both Pb(OAc)₄ and HIO₄ give same cleavage products; HIO₄ is preferred for aqueous conditions; Pb(OAc)₄ used in anhydrous (acetic acid) conditions

edges.notes: Vicinal diol (1,2-diol) oxidative cleavage with HIO4 (periodic acid): R-C(OH)(R1)-C(OH)(R2)-R' → R-CO-R1 + R2CO-R' (TWO SEPARATE carbonyl compounds via C-C bond cleavage); butane-2,3-diol (CH3-C(OH)H-C(OH)H-CH3) + HIO4 → 2x ethanal (CH3CHO); ALSO: Pb(OAc)4 (lead tetraacetate) gives SAME result as HIO4; RULE: the substituents on each diol carbon become the substituents on the resulting carbonyl; OH-C(R)(R1) → R-CO-R1; HIO4 ONLY works on VICINAL (adjacent, 1,2-diol) — does NOT cleave 1,3-diol or non-adjacent diols; contrast PINACOL rearrangement (H+) = gives ONE rearranged ketone (no cleavage, 1,2-shift) | Pb(OAc)₄ (lead tetraacetate) oxidative cleavage of vicinal diol: propane-1,2-diol (CH₃-CH(OH)-CH₂OH, 1,2-diol) + Pb(OAc)₄ → ethanal (CH₃CHO) + formaldehyde (HCHO) + Pb(OAc)₂ + 2 AcOH; GENERAL: 1,2-diol (vicinal diol) + Pb(OAc)₄ → two carbonyl compounds (one from each C of the former C-C-diol) + Pb(OAc)₂ + 2 AcOH; PRODUCT RULE: each C of the diol → C=O (aldehyde if that C had H; ketone if no H); if R-CH(OH)-CH(OH)-R' → R-CHO + R'-CHO; if R₂C(OH)-C(OH)R₂ → R₂C=O + R₂C=O (two ketones); MECHANISM: BOTH OH groups of the 1,2-diol simultaneously esterify with Pb(OAc)₄ → cyclic 5-membered Pb diester intermediate (Pb coordinated to both O) → C-C bond homolysis/heterolysis → two C=O + Pb(OAc)₂ (Pb reduced from +4 to +2); STEREOCHEMICAL REQUIREMENT: SYN (cis) diols form the cyclic 5-membered TS more readily; ANTI diols on rigid ring systems (trans-cyclohexane-1,2-diol where both OH are axial/trans) CANNOT form the cyclic Pb ester → do NOT react via the cyclic mechanism → lower reactivity; COMPARISON WITH HIO₄ (periodic acid): HIO₄ also cleaves 1,2-diols via a cyclic mechanism; both Pb(OAc)₄ and HIO₄ give same cleavage products; HIO₄ is preferred for aqueous conditions; Pb(OAc)₄ used in anhydrous (acetic acid) conditions

edges.conditions: Conceptual isomerism comparison; no actual reaction at RT (C-C bond cleavage requires >300°C). These are constitutional (structural) isomers: pentane (n-C₅H₁₂, straight chain) and 2,2-dimethylpropane (neopentane, branched C₅H₁₂). Both C₅H₁₂ molecular formula, different connectivity. Boiling points: pentane 36°C; neopentane 9.5°C (more branching → less surface area → lower bp). Thermodynamic stability: more branched isomer is more stable (heats of combustion: neopentane < pentane).
edges.notes: Chain isomers (skeletal isomers): same molecular formula, different carbon skeleton (backbone arrangement); DEFINITION: change the branching pattern of the C-C bonds; EXAMPLES for C5H12: (1) pentane (n-pentane, unbranched 5-carbon chain); (2) 2-methylbutane (isopentane: 4C main chain + 1C branch at C2); (3) 2,2-dimethylpropane (neopentane: 3C chain + 2 methyls at C2); → 3 chain isomers total for C5H12; EXAMPLES for cyclic C6H12: cyclohexane (6C ring), methylcyclopentane (5C ring + 1C side), 1,1-dimethylcyclobutane and 1,2-dimethylcyclobutane and ethylcyclobutane (4C ring variants), and cyclopropane variants (3C ring + chain); RULE: when counting chain isomers, place functional groups at the end of each carbon skeleton; KEY: chain isomers have same: (a) molecular formula MF; (b) number of C and H; different: carbon skeleton shape (linear vs branched vs ring size); COUNTING METHOD: enumerate all distinct carbon skeletons for the given total C count; for a given skeleton, the functional group must then be placed at a specific position

edges.conditions: hν (UV/visible light); gas phase or CCl₄ solvent; room temperature. Radical chain: initiation X₂+hν→2X•; propagation X•+R-H→HX+R•, R•+X₂→RX+X•; termination by radical coupling. Cl₂ less selective than Br₂ (ΔH‡ earlier, less selective TS). All C-H bonds compete; tertiary>secondary>primary selectivity. Br₂ selectivity: 3°:2°:1° ≈ 1600:82:1.

reagent_1.conditions: Cl₂; hν (UV light); gas phase or CCl₄; RT. Radical chlorination: selectivity for tertiary C-H (3° C-H is weakest, radical most stable). 2-Methylpropane has 9 equivalent 1° H and 1 3° H; selectivity factor for Cl: 3°:1° ≈ 5:1; statistical factor: 1H×5 : 9H×1 = 5:9 = ~36% 3° product. Predominantly 2-c
reagent_1.note: Free radical substitution (FSR); 2-methylpropane (isobutane) + Cl2/hv → 2-chloro-2-methylpropane (major) + 1-chloro-2-methylpropane (minor); mechanism: ①initiation: Cl2+hv→2Cl•; ②propagation: (CH3)3CH + Cl• → (CH3)3C• + HCl; (CH3)3C• + Cl2 → (CH3)3CCl + Cl•; ③termination: R•+Cl•→RCl; 3° radical more stable → 3° product major; rate order: 3° > 2° > 1°

reagent_2.conditions: Br₂; hν; CCl₄ or gas phase; RT. Radical bromination: very high selectivity for 3° C-H. Br• is selective (high ΔH‡, late TS resembles product radical → most stable radical reacts). 3°:1° selectivity ≈ 1600:1. Product: almost exclusively 2-bromo-2-methylpropane (t-BuBr). Compare: Cl₂/hν → mixture (low
reagent_2.note: FSR bromination; Br2 is MOST SELECTIVE halogen for FSR (F2 least selective); 2-methylpropane + Br2/hv → ONLY 2-bromo-2-methylpropane (nearly 100% 3° selectivity for Br2); rationale: Br• is a weak H-abstractor (endothermic step) so it is VERY selective for the most stable (3°) radical; Cl• is less selective (exothermic, more product-like TS); selectivity order: Br2 > Cl2 > F2 (inversely related to reactivity)

reagent_3.conditions: Br₂; hν; RT; CCl₄ or gas phase. Radical bromination: 2° C-H preferred over 1° (selectivity 2°:1° ≈ 82:1). Propane: 6 equivalent 1° H and 2 equivalent 2° H. Statistical × selectivity → 2-bromopropane major (secondary). Product: 2-bromopropane predominantly.
reagent_3.note: FSR bromination of propane; propane has 6 equivalent 1° H and 2 equivalent 2° H; with Br2/hv: 2-bromopropane (secondary) is major (>95%) even though only 2 H are 2°; Br• is so selective it preferentially abstracts the 2° H over the 1° H; contrast Cl2/hv with propane: gives ~55% 2-chloropropane + 45% 1-chloropropane (much less selective); selectivity difference is the key JEE concept

reagent_4.conditions: Cl₂; hν; RT; gas phase or CCl₄. Radical chlorination: lower selectivity than Br₂. 2°:1° selectivity ≈ 3.5:1. Propane: 6×1° + 2×2° H. Product ratio: 2-chloro : 1-chloro ≈ 2×3.5 : 6×1 = 7:6. 2-Chloropropane slightly major. Statistical correction required. Mixture of 1-chloropropane + 2-chloropropane.
reagent_4.note: FSR chlorination of propane; Cl2/hv → mixture of 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane; 2-chloropropane is still major (2° radical more stable) but 1-chloropropane also forms in significant amount (~45%); Cl• is less selective than Br• because H-abstraction by Cl• is highly exothermic (early TS, resembles reactant, doesn't care much about radical stability); with Br2, 2-bromopropane >95% (Br• is late TS, resembles product radical)

reagent_5.conditions: Conc HNO₃ (dilute nitric acid, 20%); 400°C; gas phase (vapour-phase nitration). Free radical mechanism at high T: HNO₃ → OH• + NO₂• at 400°C; OH• abstracts H from propane → propyl radical → NO₂• + radical → nitropropane. 2-Nitropropane (secondary, from more stable 2° radical) is major product. 1-Nit
reagent_5.note: Vapour-phase nitration of propane; propane + conc HNO3/400°C → mixture: 2-nitropropane (major, 2° H abstracted preferentially by NO2•) + 1-nitropropane (minor) + nitroethane + nitromethane (C-C bond cleavage at 400°C gives shorter-chain nitroalkanes as byproducts); assumed mechanism: free radical; rate order: 3° > 2° > 1°; high temperature (400°C) causes both C-H and C-C homolysis → multiple products; contrast with aromatic nitration (electrophilic, not free radical)

selectivity difference is the key JEE concept | FSR chlorination of propane; Cl2/hv → mixture of 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane; 2-chloropropane is still major (2° radical more stable) but 1-chloropropane also forms in significant amount (~45%); edges.conditions: Al₂O₃-Cr₂O₃ catalyst (dehydrocyclisation/aromatisation); 600°C; H₂ atmosphere (or H₂ removed). Catalytic reforming (platforming): linear hexane → dehydrogenation → cyclohexane → dehydrogenation → benzene. Or: Pd/charcoal + Δ (cyclohexane → benzene, simpler). Industrial: catalytic reforming of naphtha → benzene. High temperature, metal oxide catalyst.
edges.notes: catalytic reforming/aromatization; also Mo2O3/delta; dehydrogenation + cyclization of alkane

edges.conditions: Kolbe intramolecular electrolysis: disodium glutarate (glutaric acid Na₂ salt); electrolysis; 5A current; Pt anode. Anode: two –COO⁻ → two carboxyl radicals → lose 2CO₂ → trimethylene diradical → ring closure → cyclopropane. Intramolecular Kolbe: adipate (n=6 dicarboxylate) → cyclobutane; glutarate (n=5) → cyclopropane; pimelate (n=7) → cyclopentane.
edges.notes: Intramolecular Kolbe electrolysis; NaOOC-(CH2)3-COONa (pentanedioate, glutarate) + 5A → both carboxylates lose CO2 → •CH2-CH2-CH2• (1,3-diradical) → intramolecular coupling → cyclopropane + 2CO2; general rule: NaOOC-(CH2)n-COONa → cycloalkane with n carbons; Q11-type: n=3 → cyclopropane; n=4 → cyclobutane; n=5 → cyclopentane
edges.exception_description: Kolbe_Schmitt_KOH_gives_para_NOT_ortho → Kolbe-Schmitt with potassium phenoxide (K⁺ counterion) at ~200°C gives para-hydroxybenzoic acid (4-hydroxybenzoic acid), NOT salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid). | Kolbe_cathode_H2_not_Na_metal → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe cathode h2 not na metal. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_CH4_from_HCOONa_not_possible → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe ch4 from hcoona not possible. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_propanoate_ester_major_not_alkane → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe propanoate ester major not alkane. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gomberg_trityl_radical_stable_from_Kolbe_triphenylacetate → Exception to the standard rule: Gomberg trityl radical stable from kolbe triphenylacetate. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Kolbe_Schmidt_gives_para_product_always → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe schmidt gives para product always. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Aqueous H₂SO₄ (dil.) or H₃O⁺; Markovnikov addition; ionic mechanism via carbocation. Step 1: H⁺ protonates alkene → more stable carbocation. Step 2: H₂O attacks → oxonium ion → deprotonation → alcohol. Gives Markovnikov product. Prone to rearrangements (1,2-hydride or 1,2-alkyl shift if more stable carbocation available). RT to 60°C.

reagent_1.conditions: Step 1 (hydroboration): RT; dry THF solvent; anhydrous conditions; BH₃·THF added to alkene → boron goes to less-substituted C (anti-Markovnikov, syn addition). Step 2 (oxidation): 0°C→RT; H₂O₂ (30%), NaOH(aq); replaces B with OH with retention of configuration. Overall: syn, anti-Markovnikov additio
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. genuine_levers: Markovnikov regiochemistry — compare carbocation stability at each carbon to determine OH placement
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: conditional — only if the two faces of the resulting sp3 carbon bear four different groups; check substituents before asserting chirality
common_traps: E/Z of the starting alkene is ERASED by addition — do not invoke it as a difficulty lever or stereochemistry hook
Tertiary carbocation formed: 'no rearrangement' note is gratuitous unless a competing migration is genuinely possible
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (regiochemistry only); Interpretive only if the formed alcohol is genuinely chiral

edges.conditions: –78°C to RT; ozonolysis in CH₂Cl₂ or MeOH; O₃ (ozonator). Reductive workup: Zn/H₂O, Zn/AcOH, PPh₃, or Me₂S → aldehydes/ketones (no further oxidation). Oxidative workup: H₂O₂ → carboxylic acids from terminal alkene; H₂O₂/AcOH → carboxylic acids. Mechanism: [3+2] cycloaddition → carbonyl oxide (molozonide) → ozonide → workup.

reagent_1.conditions: O₃; Zn/H₂O (reductive); –78°C; CH₂Cl₂. 1-Methylidenebutane (CH₂=CH-CH₂-CH₂-CH₃, 1-pentene terminal alkene? Or exo-methylene: CH₂=C(chain)). Terminal alkene CH₂= → HCHO (formaldehyde) + R-CHO (butanal) after reductive workup. If 1-methylidenebutane is CH₂=CHCH₂CH₂CH₃ (pent-1-ene): ozonolysis → HCHO +
reagent_1.note: Reductive ozonolysis of a terminal alkene (R-CH=CH2); general: R-CH=CH2 + O3/Zn/H2O → R-CHO (aldehyde) + HCHO (formaldehyde/methanal); for terminal alkene, the =CH2 end gives HCHO; the internal end gives R-CHO; under reductive conditions (Zn or DMS), HCHO is the product from terminal carbon; under OXIDATIVE conditions (H2O2): HCHO → HCOOH (formic acid); NOTE: all reductive ozonolysis products are aldehydes/ketones (NEVER carboxylic acid)

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'II'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Reductive ozonolysis of a terminal alkene (R-CH=CH2); general: R-CH=CH2 + O3/Zn/H2O → R-CHO (aldehyde) + HCHO (formaldehyde/methanal); for terminal alkene, the =CH2 end gives HCHO; the internal end gives R-CHO; under reductive conditions (Zn or DMS), HCHO is the product from terminal carbon; under OXIDATIVE conditions (H2O2): HCHO → HCOOH (formic acid); NOTE: all reductive ozonolysis products are aldehydes/ketones (NEVER carboxylic acid)

genuine_levers: Reductive ozonolysis (Zn/H2O) gives aldehyde from terminal alkene C=CH2; internal symmetrical alkene gives one aldehyde (doubled); internal unsymmetrical gives two different aldehydes
common_traps: E/Z of starting alkene is ERASED by ozonolysis — do not invoke
Oxidative workup (H2O2) gives carboxylic acid, not aldehyde — workup condition is a genuine lever
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Structural (which C=O fragments appear); Interpretive if oxidative vs reductive workup is the discriminator

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; 1–3 atm H₂; heterogeneous Pd/C, Pt, Ni or Raney-Ni catalyst. Syn addition: both H atoms delivered to same face from catalyst surface. Ethanol or ethyl acetate solvent typical. Degree of substitution slows rate (more hindered alkene → slower). Raney-Ni requires no high-pressure apparatus.

reagents: Pt (or Pd or Ni or Raney Ni or PtO2 or Pd-C)
reagent_1.conditions: H₂; Pt (or Pd, Ni, Raney Ni); RT to 50°C; EtOH solvent; 1–3 atm. Heterogeneous catalytic hydrogenation; syn addition of H₂ from catalyst surface. Cis and trans but-2-ene both give butane (geometry of C=C erased after full reduction). Degree of substitution: internal alkene reacts at comparable rate
reagent_1.note: Catalytic hydrogenation; alkene + H2/metal → alkane; SYN addition (both H delivered from same face of alkene, via adsorption on metal surface); NO rearrangement (no carbocation); exothermic (ΔH<0); catalysts: Pt, Pd, Ni, Raney Ni, PtO2 (Adam's catalyst), [Rh(PPh3)3Cl] (Wilkinson's catalyst), Pd-C, Pt-C

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Catalytic hydrogenation; alkene + H2/metal → alkane; SYN addition (both H delivered from same face of alkene, via adsorption on metal surface); NO rearrangement (no carbocation); exothermic (ΔH<0); catalysts: Pt, Pd, Ni, Raney Ni, PtO2 (Adam's catalyst), [Rh(PPh3)3Cl] (Wilkinson's catalyst), Pd-C, Pt-C

edges.conditions: RT or 0°C; polar non-protic solvent (CH₂Cl₂) or neat; ionic mechanism. Step 1: H⁺ protonates alkene at less-substituted C → more stable carbocation (Markovnikov). Step 2: X⁻ attacks carbocation (no face selectivity → racemic at new stereocentre). Reactivity order HX: HI>HBr>HCl. More substituted alkene = faster (more stable carbocation intermediate).

reagent_1.conditions: RT; non-polar or CCl₄ solvent; radical chain mechanism initiated by peroxide (ROOR) thermolysis or hν. Peroxide→RO•→adds to less-substituted C of alkene (anti-Markovnikov: avoids steric strain at radical) → secondary/tertiary radical → Br• transfer from HBr. Only HBr works (HCl and HI do NOT give an
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: RT or 0°C; neat or polar aprotic solvent; HX (HCl, HBr, HI) ionic addition. Step 1: H⁺ adds to less-substituted C (Markovnikov) → carbocation. Step 2: X⁻ attacks. Reactivity: HI > HBr > HCl. Rearrangements possible for certain skeletons. For iodination (HI): often cleanest Markovnikov; anti-Markovni
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions: Radical allylic bromination: NBS (N-bromosuccinimide), hν or AIBN initiator; CCl₄ solvent; reflux or heat. Mechanism: Br• abstracts allylic H (weakest C-H due to radical stabilisation) → resonance-stabilised allylic radical → Br₂ (from NBS) → allylic bromide. Wohl-Ziegler reaction (NBS). Selectivity
reagent_3.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_4.conditions: Radical addition: HBr + peroxide (ROOR) or hν; non-polar solvent; RT. Peroxide → RO• → abstracts H from HBr → Br•; Br• adds to less-substituted C (anti-Markovnikov, avoids steric at Br; secondary radical intermediate more stable) → C-radical → abstracts H from HBr → anti-Markovnikov product. ONLY HB
reagent_4.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Hot acidic KMnO₄ (concentrated, excess); heat (Δ); aqueous H₂SO₄. Oxidative cleavage: internal alkene → 2 carboxylic acid fragments. (E)-but-2-ene (CH₃CH=CHCH₃) + hot KMnO₄ → 2 CH₃COOH (ethanoic acid × 2). Terminal alkene → HCOOH (then CO₂ with excess KMnO₄). Hot concentrated acidic KMnO₄ = strong oxidant (cold dilute = syn diol; hot conc = cleavage).

reagent_1.conditions: Koch reaction: CO + H₂O + H₂SO₄ (cat.); isobutylene (2-methylpropene) under CO pressure; 0°C to RT. Mechanism: H₂SO₄ protonates isobutylene → t-Bu⁺ (stable 3° carbocation) → CO (nucleophile trapping carbocation) → acylium t-BuCO⁺ → H₂O → pivalic acid (2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid). Also called Koch-Ha
reagent_1.note: Koch reaction: 2-methylprop-1-ene (isobutylene, (CH3)2C=CH2) + CO + H2O → 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid (pivalic acid, (CH3)3C-COOH) in the presence of H2SO4; MECHANISM (three steps): (1) H+ (from H2SO4) adds to alkene via Markovnikov → tert carbocation (CH3)3C+ (tert-butyl cation); (2) the tertiary carbocation attacks CO (carbon monoxide) → ACYLIUM ION (CH3)3C-C+=O; acylium ion is stabilized by the positive charge on C= O (formally R-C≡O+); (3) acylium ion + H2O → H2O attacks the electrophilic C+ → tetrahedral intermediate → -H+ → (CH3)3C-COOH (carboxylic acid); PRODUCT: 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid (tertiary carboxylic acid); KEY: Koch reaction always gives MARKOVNIKOV-type product (the COOH attaches to the MORE substituted carbon, which was the carbocation site); more substituted alkenes give more stable carbocations → more efficient Koch reaction; general: alkene + CO + H2O (H+) → carboxylic acid with COOH at most-substituted carbon

edges.notes: Alkene oxidative cleavage: (E)-but-2-ene (CH3-CH=CH-CH3) + hot KMnO4 → 2 × ethanoic acid (CH3COOH); GENERAL: R-CH=CH-R' + hot KMnO4 → R-COOH + R'-COOH (two carboxylic acids; C=C bond is cleaved oxidatively); ALSO: O3/oxidative workup (O3 then H2O2 or KMnO4) → same products; COMPARE: cold dilute KMnO4 (Baeyer's reagent) → diol (syn-dihydroxylation, NO cleavage); reductive ozonolysis (O3/Zn, H2O or O3/Me2S) → aldehydes (R-CHO) not acids; TERMINAL alkene (R-CH=CH2): H2C= side → CO2 + H2O (formaldehyde gets further oxidized to CO2 under hot KMnO4); only R- side gives R-COOH; INTERNAL alkene: each R group becomes RCOOH; KEY: hot acidic KMnO4 = CLEAVAGE of double bond; both C=C carbons get oxidized to COOH | Koch reaction: 2-methylprop-1-ene (isobutylene, (CH3)2C=CH2) + CO + H2O → 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid (pivalic acid, (CH3)3C-COOH) in the presence of H2SO4; MECHANISM (three steps): (1) H+ (from H2SO4) adds to alkene via Markovnikov → tert carbocation (CH3)3C+ (tert-butyl cation); (2) the tertiary carbocation attacks CO (carbon monoxide) → ACYLIUM ION (CH3)3C-C+=O; acylium ion is stabilized by the positive charge on C= O (formally R-C≡O+); (3) acylium ion + H2O → H2O attacks the electrophilic C+ → tetrahedral intermediate → -H+ → (CH3)3C-COOH (carboxylic acid); PRODUCT: 2,2-dimethylpropanoic acid (tertiary carboxylic acid); KEY: Koch reaction always gives MARKOVNIKOV-type product (the COOH attaches to the MORE substituted carbon, which was the carbocation site); more substituted alkenes give more stable carbocations → more efficient Koch reaction; general: alkene + CO + H2O (H+) → carboxylic acid with COOH at most-substituted carbon
edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only | Oxidation of primary amine RCH2NH2 with KMnO4 gives RNO2 → RCH2NH2 + KMnO4 → RCHO → RCOOH (over-oxidation path); tertiary primary amine R3CNH2 gives R3C-NO2 | The rate of intramolecular SN2 cyclization increases as ring size increases (larger rings form more slowly due to entropy loss, so smaller rings form faster), giving order: 3 > 4 > 5 > 6 > 7 → Rate order is: 6 > 5 > 3 > 7 > 4. The 4-membered ring is the SLOWEST (not the largest ring). The 6-membered ring is the FASTEST (not the 3-membered). The 3-membered ring (aziridine) forms FASTER than the 4-membered ring (azetidine) despite being more strained. Teacher's Fun Fact (page 3, yellow data): n=4 (6-membered ring) = 100, n=1 (3-membered) = 7, n=2 (4-membered) = 1, n=5 (7-membered) = 2. The 4-membered ring is exceptionally slow because the cyclobutane-like TS has extreme angle strain (Baeyer strain ~ 90° bond angles). The 6-membered ring is fastest because the chair-like TS has no ring strain AND near-ideal SN2 geometry (N···C-Br angle ~170°). The teacher's teacher 'Rate of intramolecular cyclisation ↓' (decreasing arrow) refers to the rate going from 6 → lower as you go to other ring sizes, not a monotonic decrease with ring size. | KMnO4_H_plus_oxidizes_primary_alcohol_to_aldehyde → Exception to the standard rule: Kmno4 h plus oxidizes primary alcohol to aldehyde. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | tert_alkylbenzene_oxidized_by_KMnO4_to_benzoic_acid → Exception to the standard rule: Tert alkylbenzene oxidized by kmno4 to benzoic acid. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | In 1,2-migration reactions, more substituted (tertiary) alkyl groups migrate FASTER than less substituted (hydrogen), following carbocation stability → In Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, HYDROGEN migrates FASTER than tertiary alkyl: the full order is H > 3° > 2° > cyclohexyl > phenyl > 1° > cyclopropyl > methyl. This is the ONLY major rearrangement where H has the highest migratory aptitude and where cyclopropyl (highly reactive in SN1) migrates SLOWER than primary alkyl. Reason: BV migration is NOT a carbocation process — it is a concerted migration toward the electrophilic O-O bond (Criegee mechanism). The C-H bond donates electrons directly into the O-O σ* orbital; C-H hyperconjugation is exceptionally effective in this geometry.
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: RT; aqueous acetone or t-BuOH/H₂O solvent; catalytic OsO₄ with NMO as co-oxidant (Upjohn conditions). OsO₄ forms cyclic osmate ester intermediate (concerted [3+2]) → syn delivery of both OH groups. Stoichiometric OsO₄ alternative uses NaHSO₃ workup. For small scale: OsO₄ (cat.)/NMO/H₂O/acetone. Product: syn (cis) diol, enantiomeric mixture for achiral alkenes.

reagent_1.conditions: RT; aqueous H₂SO₄ or dil KMnO₄/cold; indirect anti-dihydroxylation via epoxide intermediate. Step 1: mCPBA→epoxide (retention). Step 2: H₃O⁺ or HO⁻ opens epoxide (SN2, backside → anti addition of OH groups). Cold dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent) gives SYN diol (not anti); anti diol requires epoxide r
reagent_1.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_2.conditions: Cold (0–5°C), dilute KMnO₄ (Baeyer's reagent, alkaline, ~1%); aqueous NaOH or neutral; syn addition. Mechanism: K₂MnO₄ cyclic ester intermediate (like osmate ester) → syn delivery of two OH. Permanganate decolorised (violet → colourless) = positive Baeyer's test for unsaturation. Gives syn diol. Hot
reagent_2.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_3.conditions: OsO₄ (cat. or stoichiometric); NMO (N-methylmorpholine-N-oxide) as cooxidant (Upjohn); aqueous acetone or t-BuOH/H₂O; RT. Cyclic osmate ester → syn addition. NaHSO₃ (aqueous, stoichiometric OsO₄) → workup → syn diol. Highly stereospecific (both OH syn). More reliable than cold KMnO₄ for complex subs
reagent_3.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_4.conditions: Step 1: mCPBA (or peracetic acid, CH₂Cl₂, RT) → epoxide. Step 2: H₃O⁺ (dil H₂SO₄, RT) or NaOH(aq, RT) → trans-diaxial opening (SN2, anti) → anti diol. Two-step process required for anti diol. Contrast: KMnO₄/OsO₄ → syn. Acid opening of epoxide: more substituted C attacked; base opening: less hindere
reagent_4.note: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

reagent_5.conditions: Step 1: Ag catalyst, O₂ (air), 200–300°C, 10–20 atm → ethylene oxide (industrial). Step 2: H₂O/H⁺ (dilute H₂SO₄, 60°C) or H₂O/base → ethylene glycol. Industrial production of ethylene glycol (antifreeze). Also: Cl₂ + H₂O → chlorohydrin → Ca(OH)₂ → ethylene oxide → glycol.
reagent_5.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

genuine_levers: Syn dihydroxylation — both OH added to same face; E-alkene → (R,S) diol (meso); Z-alkene → (R,R)/(S,S) diol (racemic)
common_traps: E/Z of starting alkene IS a genuine lever — it determines meso vs chiral diol
Anti dihydroxylation (Br2/H2O → halohydrin → epoxide → diol) gives the opposite stereochemistry
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive — one of the few places where E/Z genuinely propagates to stereochemistry

edges.conditions: RT; CH₂Cl₂ solvent; mCPBA (meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid) or peracetic acid; concerted [1+2] cycloaddition (butterfly TS). Retention of alkene geometry (cis-alkene→cis-epoxide; trans-alkene→trans-epoxide). More electron-rich alkene reacts faster. Product is strained 3-membered ring (epoxide). mCPBA stored cold, handled with care (oxidising solid).
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.

genuine_levers: Syn epoxidation — both O atoms add to same face; retention of alkene geometry in product
difficulty_notes.creates_stereocenters: always — epoxidation of a prochiral alkene gives two new stereocenters (cis/trans epoxide from E/Z alkene)
common_traps: E-alkene → trans-epoxide (substituents remain trans); Z-alkene → cis-epoxide
E/Z of starting alkene IS a genuine lever here — it determines epoxide relative stereochemistry
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (stereochemistry of epoxide from E/Z alkene is a legitimate hard question)

edges.conditions: Step 1 (oxymercuration): RT; aqueous ROH or ROH/THF; Hg(OAc)₂ or Hg(OCOCF₃)₂; Markovnikov regioselective addition. Step 2 (demercuration): RT; NaBH₄; NaOH(aq) → replace Hg with H. Overall: anti-Markovnikov? No — Markovnikov: OH (as OR) at more-substituted C, H at less substituted. No rearrangements. Gives mixed ether (ROH as nucleophile in oxymercuration).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | When an epoxide with one tertiary carbon is opened under acidic conditions, the nucleophile attacks the LESS substituted (less hindered, primary) carbon, following the standard SN2 rule for nucleophilic attack on the less hindered site → Under ACIDIC conditions, epoxide opening is SN1-TYPE: the nucleophile attacks the MORE SUBSTITUTED (tertiary) carbon, NOT the less substituted. REASON: H+ protonates the epoxide O → the C-O bond at the more substituted carbon is more elongated (developing C+ character at the more stable 3° position) → the 3° C becomes the electrophilic site → Nu attacks 3° C (despite being more crowded). PRODUCT: Nu at 3° C + OH at 1° C. This is the OPPOSITE of basic epoxide opening (which follows SN2 → Nu at 1° C + OH at 3° C). MEMORY: ACID = Nu goes to MORE substituted C (Markovnikov-like for the Nu placement); BASE = Nu goes to LESS substituted C (anti-Markovnikov-like, SN2).
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: RT; aqueous Br₂ (Br₂/H₂O); anti addition. Step 1: Br⁺ (electrophile) attacks alkene π-bond → bridged bromonium ion intermediate. Step 2: H₂O (better nucleophile than Br⁻ in aqueous) attacks bromonium at more substituted C (anti, SN2-like) → trans-bromohydrin. Product: β-bromo alcohol with OH at more substituted C and Br at less substituted C (when H₂O is solvent).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: RT; non-polar solvent (CCl₄ or CH₂Cl₂); anti addition. Step 1: X₂ (X=Cl,Br) polarised → X⁺ attacks alkene → bridged halonium ion. Step 2: X⁻ attacks from opposite face (SN2 anti) → trans-dihalide. cis-alkene → anti product (threo); trans-alkene → anti product (erythro). Br₂/CCl₄ reaction decolorisation is test for alkene unsaturation.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Reflux; aqueous NaOH or KOH (aq.); SN2 for primary/secondary R-X (inversion of configuration). Tertiary R-X undergoes E2 (elimination preferred under basic aq. conditions). Mechanism: HO⁻ attacks back-face of C-X → inversion. Solvent: H₂O or H₂O/EtOH mixture. Primary > secondary for SN2; tertiary → mostly elimination. SN2 rate: MeX > 1° > 2° >> 3°.

reagent_1.conditions: Acetone + NaOH (base); RT; CCl₃⁻ (carbanion from CHCl₃/base) adds to acetone C=O → CCl₃-C(CH₃)₂-O⁻ → protonation → chloretone (2,2,2-trichloro-1,1-dimethylethanol). Mechanism: CHCl₃ + NaOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene) → hydration? Or CHCl₃ → CCl₃⁻ (carbanion) → nucleophilic addition to acetone. Product
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: Polar protic solvent required (H₂O, R-OH, R-COOH). Secondary or tertiary substrate (2° gives mix of SN1 and E1; 3° gives SN1 predominantly). Weak/moderate nucleophile, dilute. High dielectric constant solvent accelerates. Temperature: moderate to high. STEREOCHEMICAL OUTCOME: near-racemic (50% inver

reagent_3.conditions: AgNO₃ in aqueous solution (polar protic). Ag⁺ ion dramatically accelerates ionization by coordinating to the departing X⁻ → forms AgX↓ (AgCl, AgBr, AgI are all insoluble). Rate r₁ (with Ag⁺) >>> r₂ (water alone) [triple inequality in notes]. The precipitation of AgX is the thermodynamic driving forc
reagent_3.note: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

reagent_4.conditions: MOIST Ag2O → ALCOHOL: Moist Ag2O in the presence of water: Ag2O + H2O ⇌ 2AgOH (equilibrium). AgOH provides: (a) Ag+ which coordinates with X in R-X → weakens C-X bond → SN1 ionization → AgX precipitates (drives reaction to completion); (b) OH- which then attacks R+ → R-OH. OR alternatively: AgOH act
reagent_4.note: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

edges.conditions: Aqueous acidic conditions (Zn/HCl or Zn/H⁺); reductive dehalogenation. Zinc metal (or Mg) provides 2 electrons: R-X + Zn + H⁺ → R-H + ZnX⁺. Alternative: LiAlH₄ (dry ether, RT). For gem-dihalides: Zn/HCl removes both halogens to give alkene, then further reduction to alkane. Used in synthesis to remove halogens introduced in HVZ or halogenation steps.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Reflux; alcoholic KOH (ethanolic KOH) or t-BuOK/t-BuOH; heat required; E2 mechanism. Anti-periplanar geometry required (H and X must be anti). Saytzeff product (more-substituted alkene) preferred with small bases (KOH/EtOH). Hofmann product preferred with bulky bases (t-BuOK). Tertiary > secondary > primary for E2 rate. Competition with SN2: polar aprotic → SN2; polar protic/base/heat → E2.

1,2 ELIMINATION (β-ELIMINATION) — DEHYDROHALOGENATION:

NOMENCLATURE:
- α-carbon = carbon bearing the leaving group (X)
- β-carbon = carbon ADJACENT to α-C, bear

edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product. | Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.

genuine_levers: alc. KOH = E2 elimination → alkene (Zaitsev product unless bulky base)
common_traps: alc. KOH gives alkene, NOT carbocation — carbocation is only a transient intermediate in E1
E2 is anti-periplanar: if substrate is cyclic, product geometry is constrained
difficulty_notes.jee_difficulty_axis: Interpretive (alc. vs aq. KOH discrimination is a classic JEE lever)

edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.

reagent_8.note: SN2 at chiral centre → Walden inversion → configuration flips R→S (or S→R); GENERAL: SN2 at (R)-substrate → (S)-product (inversion); CIP R/S ASSIGNMENT PROCEDURE: (1) assign priorities a>b>c>d to 4 substituents using CIP Sequence Rules; (2) orient molecule so group d (LOWEST priority) points AWAY from viewer (into page, dash bond); (3) count a→b→c: CLOCKWISE = R; ANTI-CLOCKWISE = S; (4) if d is pointing TOWARD viewer (wedge), do step 3 then REVERSE (R↔S); CIP SEQUENCE RULE 1 (most used): higher atomic number (Z) = higher priority; tie → go to next attached atoms (outward exploration); tie broken at FIRST point of difference; SEQUENCE RULE 2 (tie-breaker for isotopes): higher mass number = higher priority (²H > ¹H); SEQUENCE RULE 3-5: for multiple bonds and stereocentres, use phantom atom rules (see phantom_atom concept)

edges.conditions:
ALKYNE SYNTHESIS BY DOUBLE ZN DEHALOGENATION (RM lec 11, page 6):

TEACHER Q18 (page 6): Br-chain-Br (dibromo or more) + Zn → 'n≡n' (= alkyne, H≡H type shown)

ROUTE:
  Option 1: 1,1,2,2-Tetrahalide (gem-gem dihalide on two adjacent C's) + 2Zn → alkyne
    R-CBr₂-CBr₂-R' + 2Zn → R-C≡C-R' + 2ZnBr₂
    Each Zn removes one pair of geminal Br's → generates one triple-bond component
    OR: removes both vicinal pairs in two steps
  Option 2: Sequential treatment
    Step 1: R-CBr₂-CBr₂-R' + Zn → R-CB
edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.
edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' But R-NH₂ also attacks R-X → 2°, 3°, and quaternary ammonium salt. Use excess NH₃ to favour primary amine. Gabriel phthalimide synthesis gives pure primary amine.

edges.conditions: Alcoholic KOH, heat (E2 elimination). Anti-periplanar geometry required. Follows Saytzeff rule (more substituted alkene preferred). Primary substrates may give some SN2. Distinguished from aq. KOH (SN2 → alcohol).

reagent_1.conditions: aq. KOH favours SN1 (dilute, polar protic, stabilises carbocation); conc. NaOH favours SN2 (concentrated nucleophile, primary substrate, less steric hinderance)
reagent_1.note: Primary R-X + conc. NaOH → SN2 → inversion at α-carbon. Tertiary R-X + aq. KOH → SN1 → carbocation → OH⁻ attack → racemisation. SN2: Walden inversion; SN1: racemic mixture. Key: aq/dilute = SN1; conc. = SN2.

edges.notes: alc. KOH → E2 → alkene (Saytzeff). aq. KOH → SN2 → alcohol. Key JEE distinction. Carbocation is the SN1/E1 intermediate for tertiary substrates in polar solvent without strong base — product is still alkene (E1) or alcohol (SN1), not isolated carbocation.

The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Many students apply Saytzeff universally

edges.conditions: Mg; dry ether; RT. 1,3-Dibromopropane + Mg → BrMg-CH₂CH₂CH₂-MgBr? No: intramolecular: one Grignard end attacks the other C-Br → cyclopropane + MgBr₂ (intramolecular SN2). Mg inserts into one C-Br → Grignard → intramolecular displacement of other Br → cyclopropane. Alternatively: Zn/ether gives same result. 1,3-dihalide with Mg → intramolecular coupling → cyclopropane.
edges.notes: 1,3-dibromopropane (Br-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-Br, n=3, α,ω-dihaloalkane) + Mg/ether → cyclopropane; MECHANISM: Mg inserts into one C-Br → Br-CH₂-CH₂-CH₂-MgBr (monoGrignard) → the carbanion at C1 does intramolecular SN2 on C3-Br (3-exo-tet ring closure) → cyclopropane + MgBr₂; RING SIZE RULE for X-(CH₂)n-X + Mg: n=3 → 3-membered ring (cyclopropane); n=4 → 4-membered ring (cyclobutane); n=5 → 5-membered ring (cyclopentane); GENERAL: number of carbons in ring = n (the number of CH₂ groups between the two halogens); LONGER CHAINS: 1,5-dihalo + Mg(1eq)/Δ → cyclopentane (heat required for longer chains to overcome kinetic barrier; ring closure is thermally assisted for 5- and 6-membered rings)
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Expecting mono-addition with esters

edges.conditions: NaOEt (sodium ethoxide); heat (reflux in EtOH). CHCl₃ + 3 NaOEt → HC(OEt)₃ (triethyl orthoformate) + 3 NaCl. Mechanism: NaOEt displaces Cl (SN2) three times on CHCl₃ → HC(OEt)₃. Anhydrous conditions. Orthoformate used as formyl-equivalent, protecting group, and in synthesis.

reagent_1.conditions: AMBIDENT NUCLEOPHILE — NO2- FROM KNO2 ATTACKS VIA OXYGEN: Nitrite ion (NO2- = O-N=O-) is ambident: it can attack from either O or N. With KNO2 (free nitrite anion), the O-end attacks R-X preferentially → gives ALKYL NITRITE R-ONO (also written R-O-N=O). The product is an ester of nitrous acid (HO-N=

edges.conditions: RT to reflux; dry aprotic solvent (DMF, DMSO, or ether); SN2. Sodium/potassium alkoxide (R'ONa/R'OK) prepared first (alcohol + Na). R'O⁻ + R-X → R-O-R' + X⁻. MUST use primary R-X (secondary gives E2; tertiary gives only E2). Best: R'ONa + CH₃I or primary alkyl halide. Dry conditions (alkoxides react with H₂O).

reagent_1.conditions: Dry Ag₂O (silver oxide): RT; SN2 on primary or secondary R-X; dry solvent (Et₂O or acetone). Ag₂O acts as base (accepts X⁻ as AgX precipitate) and O-nucleophile source. 2 R-X + Ag₂O → R-O-R + 2 AgX↓. Mild, anhydrous. Good for simple symmetrical ethers from 1° alkyl halides. Compare: Williamson (alko

edges.conditions: Anhydrous, inert atmosphere (N₂ or Ar); dry ether (diethyl ether) or dry THF solvent; Mg metal turnings; RT or gentle warming. Must exclude moisture strictly (Grignard reacts immediately with H₂O). Mg inserts into C-X bond: R-X + Mg(0) → R-Mg-X. Reactivity order: R-I > R-Br > R-Cl >> R-F. Vinyl and aryl halides require THF (higher bp) and sometimes I₂ activation of Mg surface.
edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

genuine_levers: Anhydrous/inert conditions are mandatory (Grignard reacts with water/O2)
tertiary Grignards suffer Wurtz coupling and elimination side reactions — not a clean textbook step for tertiary halides
common_traps: Grignard carbon bears formal carbanion character — do not confuse with SN2 product
Tertiary Grignards are experimentally problematic; prefer primary/secondary for clean chain synthesis

edges.conditions: DMSO, heat (Kornblum oxidation). Only activated (benzylic or allylic) alkyl halides. Product is aldehyde (not ketone). Mechanism: DMSO acts as oxidant via sulfonium ylide intermediate.
edges.notes: Kornblum oxidation: R-CH2-X + DMSO → R-CHO. Restricted to benzylic/allylic substrates. Primary halides give aldehydes; secondary halides give ketones only if substrate is allylic/benzylic — general alkyl halides do NOT react.

edges.conditions: Reflux; DMSO or DMF solvent (polar aprotic); NaCN or KCN. SN2 mechanism: CN⁻ attacks back-face of primary/secondary C-X → R-CN (nitrile, inversion). Ambident nucleophile: C-attack (CN⁻) preferred in polar aprotic → nitrile. N-attack (isocyanide R-NC) minor product. Primary and secondary R-X only; tertiary → E2. Chain length increases by 1C. Product used for homologation.

reagent_1.conditions: AMBIDENT NUCLEOPHILE — CN- FROM KCN ATTACKS VIA CARBON: KCN dissociates in polar solvents to give the free CN- anion. In CN-, both C and N have lone pairs, but the C-end is the kinetically preferred nucleophile (carbon is softer, better HOMO match for SN2 with R-X; also C-end has more electron densi
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: AMBIDENT NUCLEOPHILE — AgCN: Ag OCCUPIES C-END, N IS THE FREE DONOR: In silver cyanide (AgCN), the Ag atom forms a covalent bond to the CARBON (Ag-C≡N, confirmed by structure). This is because Ag+ is a soft metal ion and preferentially bonds to the softer carbon. Therefore, the NITROGEN lone pair is
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: AMBIDENT NUCLEOPHILE — AgNO2: Ag OCCUPIES O-END, N IS FREE DONOR: In silver nitrite (AgNO2), Ag is covalently bonded to the OXYGEN (Ag-O-N=O, structure verified). This leaves the NITROGEN lone pair free. R-X reacts with the N-end → R-NO2 (nitroalkane = nitro compound). Mechanism: N of Ag-O-N=O attacks R-X → R-N+(=O)(O-Ag) intermediate → AgX precipitates (driving force) → R-N+(=O)O- (zwitterionic nitro group) = R-NO2 (nitroalkane). Products: primary nitroalkanes R-NO2 from primary R-X. IUPAC: nit
