reagents: C2H5O-/Delta in C2H5OD (diagnostic: D exchange observed)
edges.conditions:
E1CB OF (2-FLUOROETHYL)BENZENE → ETHENYLBENZENE (STYRENE) (RM lec9, pages 11-12):

SUBSTRATE: Ph-CH₂-CH₂-F = (2-fluoroethyl)benzene (IUPAC)
  The Ph group (phenyl) at the β-C acts as the EWG (-M group: through resonance, carbanion at benzylic position is stabilized by delocalization into the benzene ring).
  F = poor leaving group (PLG: C-F bond is very strong = ~485 kJ/mol; F⁻ is poor LG in both E2 and SN2, but works as LG in E1CB because the carbanion forms FIRST in the slow-step).

MECHANISM:
edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.conditions:
CARBENE GENERATION FROM DIAZOMETHANE AND KETENE:

① DIAZOMETHANE (CH₂=N⁺=N⁻:) + UV → :CH₂ (methylene) + :N≡N: (nitrogen gas)
Mechanism: UV energy breaks the C=N bond of diazomethane → :CH₂ (methylene carbene) + N₂ (gas, very stable → driving force = N₂ evolution).
N₂ = EXCELLENT leaving group: N₂ is a stable gas (entropy driven) → the 1,1 elimination (H,H not actually leaving; the N₂ departs from CH₂-N₂) gives methylene.
Note: 1,1 elimination here = the N₂ group departs from the same C that had
edges.notes: Elimination: specify Saytzeff (small base, more substituted) vs Hofmann (bulky base/Hofmann exhaustive/N-oxide, less substituted) product.
edges.exception_description: Elimination gives more substituted alkene (Saytzeff) → Bulky base (t-BuOK) or quaternary ammonium (Hofmann) gives less substituted alkene | ThO₂ dehydration of alcohol gives Saytzeff product (more substituted alkene) → ThO₂/Δ gives Hofmann product (least substituted alkene, E1cb mechanism) | E2 always gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as the major product, even for rigid cyclic substrates, because the product-like TS (Hammond, DeltaH=+ve) always favors the more stable (more substituted) alkene → In RIGID CYCLIC substrates, the E2 product is determined by ANTI-PERIPLANAR geometry (which β-H is trans-diaxial to LG in the fixed conformation), NOT by Saytzeff. Teacher explicitly BOXED the rule: 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff.' When anti-periplanar and Saytzeff conflict (the trans-diaxial β-H is on the LESS substituted side), the anti-periplanar product is the actual E2 product — even if it is the less substituted alkene. Saytzeff applies ONLY for acyclic substrates (where free rotation allows all β-H to be anti-periplanar → thermodynamic product selectivity → more stable alkene is preferred). In cyclic/rigid systems: geometry (anti-periplanar) overrides thermodynamics (Saytzeff). | For a substrate with a cyclopentyl group and a chain attached to the alpha-C bearing LG, elimination toward the chain (giving more products: E+Z isomers = 2) is the major pathway because more products are formed from that side, indicating a more favorable pathway → The NUMBER OF PRODUCTS from a β-C is NOT related to whether that pathway is major. From Q9: chain pathway gives ②+② (2 products, E and Z) but EACH is formed in MINOR amount. The endocyclic pathway gives ① (only 1 product) but that 1 product is MAJOR (labeled 'S' = Saytzeff by teacher). The Saytzeff rule determines major product: more substituted alkene = major. The endocyclic alkene (ring C=C) is MORE SUBSTITUTED than the chain alkene → endocyclic = Saytzeff = major = circled-①. The chain gives E+Z (circled-② × 2) = minor × 2. So: more products ≠ major pathway. Substitution level of the alkene (not the number of stereoisomers) determines major product. | In E2 elimination, the major product is always the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff), regardless of whether one possible product is conjugated or resonance-stabilized, because Saytzeff rule is about thermodynamic stability of the alkene product → When one possible E2 product is CONJUGATED (resonance-stabilized), the conjugated alkene is the MAJOR product even if it is LESS SUBSTITUTED than the Saytzeff alkene. Teacher's BOXED rule: 'Resonance > Sytzeff.' Resonance (conjugation) provides stabilization that outweighs the hyperconjugative stabilization from extra alkyl substitution. Since E2 TS is product-like (ΔH=+ve, Hammond), the more stable product alkene = lower TS energy = faster E2 = major. Conjugated alkene stability (extended π delocalization) > Saytzeff alkene stability (simple substitution) in these cases. The full hierarchy: Anti-periplanar > Resonance > Saytzeff. | In cyclohexyl E2 problems, after identifying that Br is axial, the major product is the Saytzeff alkene (most substituted = most substituted by ring substitution or endocyclic alkene) from the most substituted beta-C → For cyclohexyl E2 with multiple substituents, the product is determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial) to the axial LG, NOT by Saytzeff substitution. From teacher's pages 4-5: Newman analysis shows some beta-C's are BLOCKED (labeled X) even when axial LG is present — because that specific beta-C has no AXIAL beta-H (only equatorial H at that beta-C position) → cannot give trans-diaxial elimination → blocked. The E2 product comes ONLY from the beta-C that has an axial beta-H anti to the axial LG. The Saytzeff beta-C may be blocked (X) while only the less-substituted (anti-Saytzeff) beta-C is accessible → the anti-periplanar product wins over Saytzeff. This is the 'Anti-elimination > Saytzeff' rule explicitly applied to cyclohexyl with blocked pathways. | In E2 reactions, the major product (Saytzeff = more substituted alkene) is determined by the substrate structure alone; the same substrate always gives the same major product regardless of base → The base SIZE changes the major product. Same substrate + small base (alc. KOH) → Saytzeff (more substituted) = major. Same substrate + bulky base (t-BuOK) → Hofmann (less substituted) = major. Two completely different products from the SAME substrate. Teacher examples (page 8): acyclic tertiary bromide + t-OO⁻/Δ → Hofmann (labeled); cyclohexyl + t-BuOK/Δ → Hofmann product (methylenecyclohexane type, labeled). The choice of base is as important as the substrate structure in determining E2 product selectivity. Complete classification: CH₃O⁻, EtO⁻, iPrO⁻, alc. KOH = Saytzeff; t-BuO⁻, LDA, DBN, DBU = Hofmann. | NR3+ (trimethylamine or similar, leaving as NMe3) is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because NMe3 is a stable neutral molecule that easily departs — similar to how Br- departs in a halide substrate → NR₃⁺ (ammonium) is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2. Despite NR₃ being a stable neutral amine molecule, the C-N bond in a quaternary ammonium is STRONG (C-N bond strength for alkylammonium is comparable to C-C) and NR₃⁺ is a poor leaving group because N⁻ would be an extremely unstable anion — so the departure requires anti-periplanar assistance. The DATA proves it: NR₃⁺ gives 18-82% Hofmann (vs Br⁻ which gives 70% Saytzeff). The Hofmann selectivity (82%) for NR₃⁺ is much higher than for Br⁻ (only 30% Hofmann) → confirms NR₃⁺ is a much poorer LG than Br⁻. Poor LG = carbanion TS = 1°>2°>3° = Hofmann. The specific leaving group ability order for E2: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ > SR₂⁺ > NR₃⁺ (decreasing LG ability = increasing Hofmann selectivity). | Fluoride is a GOOD leaving group in E2 because F- is a stable anion (highly electronegative, stable as F- with full octets and small size = most stable anion); E2 with C-F substrate should give the fastest rate and most Saytzeff product → F⁻ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP in E2 — the WORST among halogens. The C-F bond is the STRONGEST C-X bond (ca. 485 kJ/mol vs C-Cl 339, C-Br 284, C-I 240 kJ/mol). Despite F⁻ being a stable anion, the strong C-F bond makes C-F → F⁻ departure very difficult. In E2, LG ability order: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ >> F⁻ (weakest LG). F gives nearly 100% Hofmann selectivity (from teacher's table, implied near row with NR₃⁺). C-F substrate is a PLG case → carbanion TS → 1° > 2° > 3° → Hofmann. Rate of E2 with C-F is slow (poor LG). In SN2: same story, C-F is the slowest alkyl fluoride. Students confuse 'F⁻ is stable' with 'F is a good LG' — stability of F⁻ doesn't compensate for the very strong C-F bond that must break. | E1 and E2 both give a mixture of regioisomers (Saytzeff + Hofmann) from the same substrate, making both reactions regioselective in the same way → E1 gives ONLY the Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene: α = 100%, ω = 0% (teacher's '#' note, page 2). E1 via H⁺ or polar protic solvent gives carbocation → carbocation collapses to give ONLY the most stable (most substituted, Saytzeff) alkene. There is NO Hofmann product from E1. This makes E1 more selective (approaches regiospecific for Saytzeff) compared to E2, which gives Saytzeff as MAJOR but also Hofmann as MINOR (= true regioselective mixture). E2 is officially 'regioselective' (gives two regioisomers, one major). E1 effectively gives only one regioisomer (Saytzeff, 100%) under H⁺ conditions. The carbocation mechanism prevents Hofmann product formation because the carbocation at α-C directs β-H loss to the most stable (most substituted) position only. | tert-Butoxide (t-BuO-) and tert-butanol (t-BuOH) both give E2 (Hofmann) because they are both derived from tert-butanol and the bulky tert-butyl group is present in both; the reagent name does not matter, only the tert-butyl group matters → t-BuO⁻ (tert-butoxide/alkoxide, the ANION = base) gives E2 (Hofmann elimination, less substituted alkene, bulky base). t-BuOH (tert-butanol, the NEUTRAL ALCOHOL = protic solvent, NOT a base) gives E1 (Saytzeff alkene, 100%, carbocation mechanism). These are COMPLETELY DIFFERENT mechanisms with COMPLETELY DIFFERENT products from the same substrate. Teacher's table (page 9): R-O⁻/Δ = E2; R-OH,Δ = E1. Teacher Q③ (page 9): tertiary iodide + t-BuOH/Δ → labeled E1 (NOT E2). The key is the protonation state: O⁻ = alkoxide = base = E2; OH = alcohol = protic solvent = E1. Even 'KOt-Bu' (potassium tert-butoxide = ionic, gives t-BuO⁻) = E2, while 't-BuOH alone' = E1. | Ester pyrolysis gives Saytzeff (more substituted) alkene as major product, just like E2 with small base (alc. KOH), because both are thermal eliminations that give the more thermodynamically stable alkene → Ester pyrolysis gives HOFMANN (less substituted) alkene as major product. Teacher: '→ Follow Hofmann rule' (underlined, page 1). The Ei mechanism (6-membered cyclic TS) gives Hofmann selectivity because the less hindered β-H is more accessible for the syn-periplanar approach within the 6-membered ring TS. This is the same selectivity as: Cope elimination (5-membered Ei), Chugaev (6-membered Ei), and E2 with bulky base (t-BuO⁻). ALL Ei mechanisms give Hofmann. Only OVERRIDE: if a conjugated (resonance-stabilized) alkene is possible → Resonance > Hofmann (teacher's Q⑥: 'Hoff 7 Reso' BOXED). The Hofmann selectivity of ester pyrolysis is particularly useful: ester pyrolysis at ~500°C gives the less substituted (terminal) alkene without any base or acid — a unique synthetic advantage. | All Ei eliminations (Cope, Chugaev, ester pyrolysis) follow Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major) regardless of whether the substrate contains a cyclopentane or cyclohexane ring → Cope and Chugaev in CYCLOPENTANE rings give SAYTZEFF (endocyclic = more substituted) as MAJOR product. Teacher (page 3): 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates.' The cyclopentane ring is nearly flat → both exo-H and endo-H can reach the cyclic Ei TS, but the endo-H TS is more planar ('Good T.S. bcoz all atoms can lie in one plane' — page 1) → endocyclic C=C (Saytzeff) is major. Teacher data: 97.5% Saytzeff from cyclopentyl N-oxide (page 1). ONLY in 6-membered rings (chair form) does Hofmann apply: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' This is a specific, named exception to the Hofmann rule for Ei reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Many students apply Saytzeff universally

edges.conditions: esterification_of_all_free_OH_groups

reagent_1.conditions: acid_or_enzyme_hydrolysis_of_glycosidic_bond
reagent_1.note: sucrose = alpha-D-glucopyranose linked to beta-D-fructofuranose via C1(glucose)-C2(fructose) glycosidic bond; BOTH anomeric carbons involved in bond -> no free anomeric OH -> non-reducing sugar; hydrolysis gives D-glucose (dextrorotatory +52.5) + D-fructose (laevorotatory -92); invert sugar: sucrose [alpha]=+66 -> mixture [alpha]=negative (fructose rotation dominates because |[alpha]_fructose| > |[alpha]_glucose|)

reagent_2.conditions: acid_or_enzyme_hydrolysis
reagent_2.note: maltose = C12H22O11; two alpha-D-glucose units linked C1(glucose1)-C4(glucose2) alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond; C1 of second glucose is FREE (anomeric OH free) -> reducing sugar; lactose = milk sugar C12H22O11; beta-D-galactose linked C1(galactose)-C4(glucose) beta-1,4-glycosidic bond; C1 of glucose free -> reducing sugar; raffinose = trisaccharide C18H32O16 -> glucose + fructose + galactose on hydrolysis

reagent_3.conditions: both_anomeric_carbons_occupied_in_alpha_1_beta_2_glycosidic_linkage
reagent_3.note: sucrose has alpha-1,beta-2-glycosidic bond between C1(alpha-glucose) and C2(beta-fructose); both C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose are tied in the bond as full acetal; neither can ring-open to give free carbonyl; sucrose does not reduce Tollens or Fehling; sucrose CANNOT mutarotate (no free anomeric OH to exchange); upon hydrolysis the released monosaccharides (glucose and fructose) ARE individually reducing

reagent_4.conditions: ketose dehydrated FASTER than aldose by HCl → 5-(hydroxymethyl)furan-2-carbaldehyde (HMF); HMF + 2 resorcinol → deep red complex via condensation
reagent_4.note: Seliwanoff's test mechanism: ketose → [H⁺, -3H₂O] → 5-(hydroxymethyl)furan-2-carbaldehyde (HMF) → [+ 2 resorcinol, H⁺] → deep cherry-red complex. Aldoses also form furfural but much more slowly → only faint pink. Sucrose gives POSITIVE because acid hydrolysis (6M HCl) cleaves it to glucose + fructose; the fructose then reacts. 6M HCl is the reagent concentration used.

edges.notes: CHO-(CHOH)4-CH2OH + 5 Ac2O -> CHO-(CH-OAc)4-CH2-OAc (glucose pentaacetate); 5 OH groups acetylated at C2,C3,C4,C5,C6; stable compound (not 2 on same C) -> all 5 OH on DIFFERENT carbons; CHO at C1 does NOT react (not an OH group in open chain) but DOES react in cyclic form; when glucose pentaacetate (cyclic) is tested with NH2OH -> no oxime -> confirms CHO now tied up in hemiacetal ring | sucrose = alpha-D-glucopyranose linked to beta-D-fructofuranose via C1(glucose)-C2(fructose) glycosidic bond; BOTH anomeric carbons involved in bond -> no free anomeric OH -> non-reducing sugar; hydrolysis gives D-glucose (dextrorotatory +52.5) + D-fructose (laevorotatory -92); invert sugar: sucrose [alpha]=+66 -> mixture [alpha]=negative (fructose rotation dominates because |[alpha]_fructose| > |[alpha]_glucose|) | maltose = C12H22O11; two alpha-D-glucose units linked C1(glucose1)-C4(glucose2) alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond; C1 of second glucose is FREE (anomeric OH free) -> reducing sugar; lactose = milk sugar C12H22O11; beta-D-galactose linked C1(galactose)-C4(glucose) beta-1,4-glycosidic bond; C1 of glucose free -> reducing sugar; raffinose = trisaccharide C18H32O16 -> glucose + fructose + galactose on hydrolysis | sucrose has alpha-1,beta-2-glycosidic bond between C1(alpha-glucose) and C2(beta-fructose); both C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose are tied in the bond as full acetal; neither can ring-open to give free carbonyl; sucrose does not reduce Tollens or Fehling; sucrose CANNOT mutarotate (no free anomeric OH to exchange); upon hydrolysis the released monosaccharides (glucose and fructose) ARE individually reducing | Seliwanoff's test mechanism: ketose → [H⁺, -3H₂O] → 5-(hydroxymethyl)furan-2-carbaldehyde (HMF) → [+ 2 resorcinol, H⁺] → deep cherry-red complex. Aldoses also form furfural but much more slowly → only faint pink. Sucrose gives POSITIVE because acid hydrolysis (6M HCl) cleaves it to glucose + fructose; the fructose then reacts. 6M HCl is the reagent concentration used.

edges.conditions: RT; neat SOCl₂ or small excess; anhydrous; mild heat if needed. SOCl₂ + RCOOH → RCOCl + SO₂ + HCl (both gases leave → irreversible). Alternative: PCl₅ (harsher), PCl₃ (milder). Oxalyl chloride (ClCOCOCl) + cat. DMF (Vilsmeier): very mild, RT for acid chloride formation. Dry conditions (acid chlorides hydrolyse readily with H₂O).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: 0°C to reflux; dry ether (diethyl ether) or THF; LiAlH₄ (anhydrous, excess 4 eq. per COOH). Mechanism: LiAlH₄ reduces COOH → aldehyde intermediate (not isolable) → primary alcohol. Cannot use NaBH₄ (too mild for carboxylic acid). Aqueous workup carefully (LiAlH₄ reacts violently with H₂O: quench with wet ether then dilute). BH₃ (milder) also reduces RCOOH → primary alcohol.

reagent_1.conditions: BH₃ (borane, B₂H₆); THF solvent; 0°C to RT; anhydrous. BH₃ reduces carboxylic acid selectively (COOH > C=O ketones/aldehydes — actually carboxylic acid reacts fastest with BH₃ among carbonyls; ketones slower). Mechanism: BH₃ coordinates to COOH oxygen → H⁻ delivery → reduction. Aqueous H₂O₂/NaOH wor
reagent_1.note: Borane (BH3) selectively reduces carboxylic acids; COOH is the EASIEST functional group for borane reduction; borane ease of reduction order: Carboxyl > Alkene > Ketone > Nitrile > Epoxide > Ester (most difficult) > Acyl Halide (INERT); KEY SELECTIVITY: borane reduces COOH easily but is INERT to acyl halide (completely opposite to other reagents); borane is used when you want to reduce COOH in presence of ester (borane reduces COOH, leaves ester intact); alkene IS reduced by borane (but via hydroboration, not simple reduction)

edges.exception_description: -BR2 (trialkylboron) groups on a carbon chain are electron donating (like alkyl groups) → When -BR2 is directly attached to a benzene ring, the empty B p-orbital (Lewis acid) accepts ring π-electrons via -M effect. Ph-BH2 behaves as an EWG, giving 5 canonical structures with positive charge on the ring (ortho/para) and negative charge on B. -BR2 on ring is a DEACTIVATOR and meta-director, opposite to its effect in hydroboration where it is electrophilic at B but the ring context reverses expectation.

edges.conditions: Soda-lime (NaOH + CaO) decarboxylation: dry heating 300–400°C → decarboxylation, R-COO⁻Na⁺ + CaO → R-H + CaCO₃ + Na₂CO₃. Dry distillation of calcium carboxylate salt (calcium salt + CaO, heat → alkane). Alternatively: Kolbe electrolysis (2RCOONa → R-R + 2CO₂ + 2NaOH at anode, gives symmetric alkane). Silver salt + Br₂/CCl₄ → Hunsdiecker reaction → R-Br (one carbon shorter).

edges.conditions: Hunsdiecker reaction: Step 1: RCOOH + AgOH (or Ag₂O) → RCOOAg (silver carboxylate). Step 2: RCOOAg + Br₂/CCl₄ → R-Br + CO₂ + AgBr. Reflux; anhydrous CCl₄; dark. Mechanism: radical. One fewer C than acid (decarboxylation). Iodide: 1:1 Ag salt:I₂ → R-I. Ester: 2:1 Ag salt:I₂ → R-OOC-R (ester). Kochi: Cu²⁺ variation.

reagents: I2/heat (1:1 molar ratio Ag salt : I2)
reagent_1.conditions: Hunsdiecker (Iodide variant): RCOOAg + I₂ (1:1 molar); anhydrous CCl₄; reflux; dark (light-sensitive). Mechanism: radical chain. AgI precipitates (insoluble) → drives equilibrium. Product: R-I (1 fewer C, decarboxylation). Primary carboxylates give primary iodides.

edges.conditions: Direct: heat RCOOH + NH₃ (or amine) → ammonium salt → strong heat → amide + H₂O. High temperature required (150–200°C); loss of H₂O. Better route: acid chloride + amine (faster, milder). DCC coupling: RCOOH + amine + DCC (RT, CH₂Cl₂) → amide + DCU (peptide synthesis). Industrial: adipic acid + hexamethylenediamine → Nylon-6,6 (amide bonds).

edges.conditions: Schmidt reaction: conc H₂SO₄; HN₃ (hydrazoic acid, generated in situ from NaN₃ + H₂SO₄); 0°C to RT. Mechanism: RCOOH protonated → acylium (RCO⁺) → HN₃ attacks → loss of N₂ → isocyanate (R-N=C=O) → H₂O → carbamic acid → CO₂ + RNH₂. One fewer C than original acid. Also works for ketones → amide (ring expansion) and for alcohols → nitrilium.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: Kolbe_Schmidt_gives_para_product_always → Exception to the standard rule: Kolbe schmidt gives para product always. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: Δ (heat alone, 200°C); intramolecular esterification. The two COOH groups (1,2-adjacent on ring) can easily cyclise to anhydride by loss of H₂O. Entropy neutral (intramolecular). No acid catalyst needed for 1,2-diacids (and some 1,4-diacids forming glutaric anhydride). Product: phthalic anhydride (used in Friedel-Crafts acylation, dye synthesis, plasticisers).

reagent_1.conditions: Heat (Δ, 200°C) or conc H₂SO₄; phthalic acid → phthalic anhydride (intramolecular, 5-membered ring with fused benzene). No catalyst needed for ortho-dicarboxylic acids (adjacent COOH easy to cyclise). Product: phthalic anhydride = 2-benzofuran-1,3-dione. Used in Friedel-Crafts acylation to make anth
reagent_1.note: Phthalic acid (benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid, ortho-phthalic acid) + Δ → phthalic anhydride (2-benzofuran-1,3-dione = isobenzofuran-1,3-dione) + H2O; ortho COOH groups on adjacent ring carbons → close proximity → dehydration → 5-membered anhydride ring fused to benzene; COMPARISON: isophthalic acid (benzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid, meta) + Δ → NO rxn (COOH too far apart for 5 or 6 membered ring); terephthalic acid (benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid, para) + Δ → NO rxn (COOH even farther); RULE: aromatic diacids → anhydride ONLY for ortho (1,2-disubstituted) isomer; meta and para give no anhydride; cis-trans analogy: ortho = like cis (close, same side); meta/para = like trans (too far)

edges.notes: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product. | Phthalic acid (benzene-1,2-dicarboxylic acid, ortho-phthalic acid) + Δ → phthalic anhydride (2-benzofuran-1,3-dione = isobenzofuran-1,3-dione) + H2O; ortho COOH groups on adjacent ring carbons → close proximity → dehydration → 5-membered anhydride ring fused to benzene; COMPARISON: isophthalic acid (benzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid, meta) + Δ → NO rxn (COOH too far apart for 5 or 6 membered ring); terephthalic acid (benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid, para) + Δ → NO rxn (COOH even farther); RULE: aromatic diacids → anhydride ONLY for ortho (1,2-disubstituted) isomer; meta and para give no anhydride; cis-trans analogy: ortho = like cis (close, same side); meta/para = like trans (too far)

edges.conditions: NaOH + CaO (soda lime); Δ (strong heat, 300–400°C); decarboxylation. PhCOONa + CaO → Ph-H + CaCO₃ (+ Na₂CO₃). Mechanism: loss of CO₂ from carboxylate anion (decarboxylation). Also: Cu powder (Ullmann decarboxylation) or Pd catalyst at lower T. Industrial decarboxylation: >200°C, base. Dry distillation of Ca salt of carboxylic acid → alkane (Kekulé synthesis equivalent for aromatic).
edges.notes: soda lime (NaOH+CaO) decarboxylation; PhCOOH → C6H6 + CO2; also works for naphthalene-COOH → naphthalene

edges.conditions: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVZ): Step 1: P (red or white) + Br₂ → PBr₃ in situ; Step 2: PBr₃ + RCOOH → acyl bromide (RCOBr) → enolisation at α-carbon → α-bromoacyl bromide → reaction with next RCOOH → α-bromo acid. Selective α-halogenation. Requires α-H. Cl₂ or Br₂ with P catalyst. Product: α-halo acid used for Gabriel, Strecker, SN2 displacement.

reagent_1.conditions: Acetic anhydride (Ac₂O) or acetyl chloride (AcCl); pyridine (catalyst/base); RT. O-acetylation of the phenolic OH of salicylic acid → aspirin (2-acetoxybenzoic acid, 2-(acetyloxy)benzoic acid). COOH not acetylated under these conditions (less nucleophilic than phenolic OH). Commercial: salicylic aci
reagent_1.note: Aspirin synthesis; salicylic acid phenol-OH esterifies with acetic anhydride or acetyl chloride → 2-acetoxybenzoic acid (aspirin); COOH remains free; pain killer / analgesic; one of the most important aromatic compound conversions in JEE

edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Aspirin synthesis; salicylic acid phenol-OH esterifies with acetic anhydride or acetyl chloride → 2-acetoxybenzoic acid (aspirin); COOH remains free; pain killer / analgesic; one of the most important aromatic compound conversions in JEE
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Gabriel synthesis can make any amine → Gabriel synthesis cannot use ArX (no SN2 on aryl halides) and only gives 1° amines | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

edges.conditions: Fischer esterification: reflux; H₂SO₄ or dry HCl catalyst; excess alcohol or remove H₂O (Dean-Stark); equilibrium. Mechanism: H⁺ protonates C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → ROH attacks → loss of H₂O → ester. Reversible; drive right by excess ROH or remove H₂O. Steric: 2,6-disubstituted (hindered) acids don't esterify well by Fischer → use DCC coupling or acyl chloride.

reagent_1.conditions: CH₂N₂ (1 equiv); RT; Et₂O or MeOH as co-solvent; immediate O-alkylation of COOH. Fastest substrate for CH₂N₂ (pKa ~5, most acidic). Product: methyl ester (R-COOCH₃). Quantitative at RT, no workup needed (N₂ evolution drives reaction). Excess CH₂N₂ methylates phenols too. Used for carbodiimide-sensit
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagents: I2/heat (2:1 molar ratio Ag salt : I2, excess Ag)
reagent_2.conditions: Hunsdiecker (Ester variant): 2 RCOOAg + I₂ (2:1 molar); anhydrous CCl₄; reflux. The second equivalent of Ag salt captures the carboxyl radical/cation → ester R-OOC-R (symmetric ester). Mechanism: first Ag salt reacts with I₂ → acyloxy radical → loses CO₂ → alkyl radical → reacts with 2nd Ag salt ins
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_5.conditions: AAc2 mechanism: (1) H₂SO₄ protonates C=O of acid → R-C(OH)₂⁺ (oxocarbenium); (2) nucleophilic attack of R'-OH at acyl carbon → tetrahedral intermediate [R-C(OH)₂(OR')H]; (3) proton transfer; (4) loss of H₂O (from original acid -OH, NOT alcohol -OR'); (5) deprotonation → ester. Bimolecular, acyl-oxyg
reagent_5.note: AAc2 = Acid-catalysed, Acyl-O cleavage, bimolecular. Key proof: ¹⁸O label on alcohol R'-¹⁸OH → ¹⁸O retained in ester (R-CO-¹⁸O-R'). This means ACYL-O bond of the acid is broken (the acid OH is lost as water), and the ALCOHOL-O bond is retained in ester. 8 possible ester hydrolysis mechanisms: [Acid: AAc1, AAc2, AAL1, AAL2] + [Base: BAc1, BAc2, BAL1, BAL2]. Most common: AAc2 (esterification), BAc2 (saponification).

edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | CO₂ gas evolution = diagnostic test for RCOOH. Sulfonic acids (R-SO₃H) also react. p-Nitrophenol (pKa≈7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ even though it's more acidic than regular phenol. | Ethanoic acid (CH3COOH) → CH3COO⁻ + H⁺; pKa = 4.76; contrast with homolytic which would give radical R-COO• + H• | AAc2 = Acid-catalysed, Acyl-O cleavage, bimolecular. Key proof: ¹⁸O label on alcohol R'-¹⁸OH → ¹⁸O retained in ester (R-CO-¹⁸O-R'). This means ACYL-O bond of the acid is broken (the acid OH is lost as water), and the ALCOHOL-O bond is retained in ester. 8 possible ester hydrolysis mechanisms: [Acid: AAc1, AAc2, AAL1, AAL2] + [Base: BAc1, BAc2, BAL1, BAL2]. Most common: AAc2 (esterification), BAc2 (saponification).

This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

edges.conditions: CH₂N₂ (diazomethane); UV (hν); ring expansion. Diazomethane + cyclopentane → cyclopentyl carbene intermediate? More likely: :CH₂ (singlet carbene from hν/CH₂N₂) inserts into C-H bond → methylcyclopentane, not cyclohexane. Actually ring expansion: cyclopentylmethyl carbene → ring expansion → cyclohexane via 1,2-H shift. UV light generates :CH₂ from CH₂N₂ → C-H insertion into cyclopentane ring → cyclohexane (ring expansion by one C).

reagent_1.conditions: Geometric isomers (cis/trans) of 1,2-dimethylcyclopropane. Ring is rigid (no free rotation about C-C in ring). Cis: both CH₃ on same face; trans: CH₃ on opposite faces. NOT interconvertible at RT (would require ring opening = breaking C-C bond, ~80 kcal/mol). Trans isomer has C₂ symmetry (chiral, tw
reagent_1.note: Geometric isomerism in cycloalkanes: the ring is RIGID (restricted rotation about ring C-C bonds) → GI possible for substituted cycloalkanes; CONDITION: each ring carbon bearing a substituent must have TWO DIFFERENT groups (the substituent and H are different → satisfied for monosubstituted carbons in the ring); CIS = both substituents on SAME FACE of the ring plane (same side = cis); TRANS = substituents on OPPOSITE FACES of ring plane; EXAMPLES: 1,2-dimethylcyclopropane: cis-1,2-dimethylcyclopropane (both Me same face) vs trans-1,2-dimethylcyclopropane (Me on opposite faces); 1,2-cyclobutanedicarboxylic acid: cis (both COOH same side) and trans (opposite); 1,2-dimethylcyclobutane: cis and trans; KEY: for 1,1-disubstituted cycloalkanes (both substituents on same C): that C has two groups to compare → if they are the SAME (e.g., 1,1-dimethyl) → no GI (C has identical groups); if different (e.g., 1-methyl-1-ethyl) → still just one carbon with different groups → still need second substituted carbon for GI comparison; GENERAL: ring system provides rigidity; GI exists when substituents can be either syn or anti relative to ring plane

edges.notes: Methylene insertion reaction; CH2N2 + UV → :CH2: (methylene carbene) + N2; :CH2: inserts into C-C bond of cyclopentane (ring expansion) → cyclohexane; ALSO :CH2: inserts into C-H bonds → methylcyclopentane (two products from cyclopentane + CH2N2/UV); both C-H insertion and C-C insertion are non-selective; ring expansion: cyclopentane (5-membered) → cyclohexane (6-membered) via C-C carbene insertion; another example: n-butane + CH2N2/UV → pentane + isopentane (C-H at 1° → pentane; C-H at 2° → isopentane; C-C insertion → methylbutane) | Geometric isomerism in cycloalkanes: the ring is RIGID (restricted rotation about ring C-C bonds) → GI possible for substituted cycloalkanes; CONDITION: each ring carbon bearing a substituent must have TWO DIFFERENT groups (the substituent and H are different → satisfied for monosubstituted carbons in the ring); CIS = both substituents on SAME FACE of the ring plane (same side = cis); TRANS = substituents on OPPOSITE FACES of ring plane; EXAMPLES: 1,2-dimethylcyclopropane: cis-1,2-dimethylcyclopropane (both Me same face) vs trans-1,2-dimethylcyclopropane (Me on opposite faces); 1,2-cyclobutanedicarboxylic acid: cis (both COOH same side) and trans (opposite); 1,2-dimethylcyclobutane: cis and trans; KEY: for 1,1-disubstituted cycloalkanes (both substituents on same C): that C has two groups to compare → if they are the SAME (e.g., 1,1-dimethyl) → no GI (C has identical groups); if different (e.g., 1-methyl-1-ethyl) → still just one carbon with different groups → still need second substituted carbon for GI comparison; GENERAL: ring system provides rigidity; GI exists when substituents can be either syn or anti relative to ring plane

edges.conditions: O₃; then Zn/H₂O (reductive workup); –78°C for ozonolysis; CH₂Cl₂ solvent. Cyclopentene → ozonide → Zn/H₂O reductive workup → ring-opened dialdehyde (pentanedial = glutaraldehyde). Cyclic alkenes give one bifunctional product (no break in chain). Zn/H₂O: reductive → aldehydes. H₂O₂: oxidative → acids.

reagent_1.conditions: O₃; then H₂O₂ (oxidative workup); –78°C, CH₂Cl₂ then H₂O₂/AcOH. Ozonolysis of cyclopentene → ozonide → H₂O₂ → pentanedioic acid (glutaric acid, 5C dicarboxylic acid). Oxidative workup: terminal CH=CH₂ → HCOOH; internal CH= → RCOOH. Ring opened completely → linear diacid.
reagent_1.note: Oxidative ozonolysis of cyclopentene; cyclopentene + O3 → ozonide → H2O2 cleaves ozonide oxidatively → pentanedioic acid (glutaric acid, HOOC-(CH2)3-COOH); oxidative workup (H2O2 or Ag2O): aldehydes are OXIDIZED to carboxylic acids; the two aldehyde groups from reductive ozonolysis are oxidized to COOH; cyclic alkene → diacid (ONE molecule, ring opened); contrast with reductive: Zn/H2O → pentane-1,5-dial (dialdehyde); H2O2 → pentanedioic acid (diacid)

edges.notes: Reductive ozonolysis of cyclopentene; cyclopentene + O3 → monozonide (C-O-O-O-C, 5-membered) → ozonide (1,2,4-trioxolane) → Zn/H2O cleaves ozonide reductively → pentane-1,5-dial (OHC-(CH2)3-CHO, glutaraldehyde); KEY: cyclic alkene gives ONE bifunctional product (the ring opens to give a linear dialdehyde, NOT two separate pieces); reductive workup (Zn or DMS) → aldehyde, not acid; Zn reduces O-O peroxide to ZnO | Oxidative ozonolysis of cyclopentene; cyclopentene + O3 → ozonide → H2O2 cleaves ozonide oxidatively → pentanedioic acid (glutaric acid, HOOC-(CH2)3-COOH); oxidative workup (H2O2 or Ag2O): aldehydes are OXIDIZED to carboxylic acids; the two aldehyde groups from reductive ozonolysis are oxidized to COOH; cyclic alkene → diacid (ONE molecule, ring opened); contrast with reductive: Zn/H2O → pentane-1,5-dial (dialdehyde); H2O2 → pentanedioic acid (diacid)
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Aqueous medium; aliphatic diazonium → instant N₂ loss even at 0°C (unlike aryl diazonium)
edges.notes: Carbocation intermediate: check for rearrangement (1,2-H or 1,2-alkyl shift) if a more stable cation is accessible.
edges.exception_description: Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Students apply SN2 mechanism to diazonium hydrolysis; mechanism is SN1 or DN+AN (phenyl cation).

edges.conditions: Sandmeyer reaction: Step 1: CuX (CuCl, CuBr, CuCN) + ArN₂⁺ → ArX (Ar-Cl, Ar-Br, Ar-CN); RT to mild heat; aqueous or aqueous-acetone. Copper(I) acts as catalyst/reagent via single-electron mechanism. Balz-Schiemann: ArN₂⁺BF₄⁻ (from HBF₄) → heat dry → ArF + N₂ + BF₃ (for fluoride). Gattermann: Cu powder (not CuX) for Cl/Br (milder alternative to Sandmeyer).

reagent_1.conditions: KI (potassium iodide); heat (Δ, warm, 50–80°C); aqueous. ArN₂⁺ + KI → Ar-I + N₂ + KCl. Iodination via diazonium: no Cu catalyst needed (I⁻ is good enough nucleophile/reductant). Mechanism: radical or ionic (ArN₂⁺ + I⁻ → Ar• + N₂ + I• → Ar-I). Direct method (no Sandmeyer needed for iodide). Gatterman
reagent_1.note: Sandmeyer-Finkelstein for ArI; benzenediazonium + KI/Δ → iodobenzene + N2 + KCl; NO Cu catalyst needed (unlike ArCl and ArBr Sandmeyer); Cu2I2 cannot be used (cuprous iodide reduces I- back); only KI/heat gives ArI directly; KI/Δ is the UNIQUE iodine Sandmeyer route

edges.notes: Temperature critical: 0–5°C for stable arenediazonium. Aliphatic diazonium decomposes immediately. Coupling requires alkaline medium. | Sandmeyer-Finkelstein for ArI; benzenediazonium + KI/Δ → iodobenzene + N2 + KCl; NO Cu catalyst needed (unlike ArCl and ArBr Sandmeyer); Cu2I2 cannot be used (cuprous iodide reduces I- back); only KI/heat gives ArI directly; KI/Δ is the UNIQUE iodine Sandmeyer route

Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems.
