After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Sandmeyer_ArI_uses_KI_directly_no_Cu_catalyst → Exception to the standard rule: Sandmeyer ari uses ki directly no cu catalyst. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gattermann_Koch_with_AlCl3_alone_sufficient → Exception to the standard rule: Gattermann koch with alcl3 alone sufficient. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Students apply SN2 mechanism to diazonium hydrolysis; mechanism is SN1 or DN+AN (phenyl cation).

edges.conditions: 0–5°C; alkaline or slightly acidic aqueous; electrophilic aromatic substitution (coupling). ArN₂⁺ (electrophile) + Ar'OH (phenol, coupling at para position, alkaline) or Ar'NH₂ (amine, coupling at para position, neutral/acidic). pH control critical: too acidic → protonates amine coupling partner (less nucleophilic). Product: azo dye Ar-N=N-Ar'. Used in azo dyes (Congo red, methyl orange, etc.).
edges.notes: Temperature critical: 0–5°C for stable arenediazonium. Aliphatic diazonium decomposes immediately. Coupling requires alkaline medium.
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

edges.conditions: 0–5°C; alkaline aqueous (NaOH or Na₂CO₃, pH 9–10); coupling at para position of β-naphthol. Electrophilic aromatic substitution: ArN₂⁺ (electrophile) + β-naphthol (highly activated ring, coupling at C-1, para to OH) → azo dye. Alkaline pH keeps naphthol as phenoxide (more nucleophilic). Acidic → protonates coupling partner → slower. Product: intensely coloured orange-red azo compound.
edges.notes: Temperature critical: 0–5°C for stable arenediazonium. Aliphatic diazonium decomposes immediately. Coupling requires alkaline medium.
edges.exception_description: Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Students apply SN2 mechanism to diazonium hydrolysis; mechanism is SN1 or DN+AN (phenyl cation).

edges.conditions: CuCN (copper cyanide) or NaCN/CuCN; RT to mild heat; aqueous. Sandmeyer reaction with cyanide. ArN₂⁺ + CuCN → ArCN + N₂. Mechanism: Cu(I) transfers CN• radical to Ar•. Product: benzonitrile (phenyl cyanide). Can also use Gattermann modification (Cu powder + HCN). Product used for further synthesis (hydrolysis → RCOOH; reduction → benzylamine).
edges.notes: Sandmeyer cyanation; benzenediazonium + NaCN (with CuCN catalyst) → benzonitrile (PhCN) + N2 + NaCl; benzonitrile = phenylcarbonitrile = masked COOH (CN can be hydrolyzed to COOH with H3O+/H+); gives ORTHO- or PARA-nitrile when starting from substituted aniline; Sandmeyer route to nitriles (cannot directly add CN to benzene without diazonium route)
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | Sandmeyer_ArI_uses_KI_directly_no_Cu_catalyst → Exception to the standard rule: Sandmeyer ari uses ki directly no cu catalyst. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gattermann_Koch_with_AlCl3_alone_sufficient → Exception to the standard rule: Gattermann koch with alcl3 alone sufficient. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para). | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

edges.conditions: Aqueous acid; heat (steam distillation conditions); H₂O. Warm/boil: ArN₂⁺ + H₂O → [Ar⁺] → ArOH + N₂. Mechanism: ArSN1-like (phenyl cation or tight ion pair). Dilute H₂SO₄ (not HCl; Cl⁻ might compete). Product: phenol. Used industrially (cumene process preferred now). Warm the diazonium salt solution gently to avoid explosive decomposition.

reagent_1.conditions: Warm aqueous acid (dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl, 60–80°C); heat ArN₂⁺ in dilute H₂SO₄ → N₂ evolution → phenol. Mechanism: ArSN1 via phenyl cation (or tight ion pair) → H₂O attacks → phenol + N₂. Do NOT use concentrated acid (risk of explosive N₂ evolution). Use dilute H₂SO₄ (not HCl — Cl⁻ competes). Temperat
reagent_1.note: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.notes: Temperature critical: 0–5°C for stable arenediazonium. Aliphatic diazonium decomposes immediately. Coupling requires alkaline medium. | EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

edges.conditions: H₃PO₂ (hypophosphorous acid) + water; RT to mild heat. Mechanism: radical → Ar• + N₂ → Ar• + H (from H₃PO₂) → ArH. Alternatively: EtOH (also reductive, gives ArH). Removes amino group from aromatic ring with no substituent replacement (deamination). Useful for: install NH₂ as ortho/para director, then remove after synthesis.
edges.notes: Temperature critical: 0–5°C for stable arenediazonium. Aliphatic diazonium decomposes immediately. Coupling requires alkaline medium.
edges.exception_description: Diazonium salts react via SN2 substitution → Aromatic diazonium salt (ArN₂⁺) hydrolysis goes via ArSN1 (phenyl cation intermediate), NOT SN2, because sp² carbon cannot accommodate back-side attack. | aliphatic diazonium R-N2+ is instantaneously unstable and cannot be isolated; aryl diazonium Ar-N2+ is stable in cold solution → Aliphatic diazonium ions R-N₂⁺ (where R = alkyl) CANNOT be isolated — they lose N₂ INSTANTLY at 0°C and above, forming carbocations. Contrast: Aryl diazonium ions Ar-N₂⁺ (where R = aryl ring) ARE stable in cold aqueous/alcoholic solution (0-5°C) and can be stored and used in subsequent reactions (Sandmeyer, azo coupling, Balz-Schiemann, etc.). Reason for difference: Aryl cation (Ar⁺, sp² C⁺) is even MORE unstable than aliphatic cation → the SN1 ionization (Ar-N₂⁺ → Ar⁺ + N₂) is kinetically retarded for aryl systems. Additionally, resonance stabilization of the aryl diazonium (Ar-N≡N⁺ ↔ Ar-N=N⁺ with charge delocalization into ring via resonance) makes Ar-N₂⁺ more stable. So: Ar-N₂⁺ stable at 0-5°C for hours; R-N₂⁺ decomposes in milliseconds. | diazotization of primary amine converts poor NH2 leaving group into N2 (the best leaving group) via diazotization → R-NH₂ cannot be used directly as an electrophile (NH₂⁻ is a terrible leaving group, pKa of NH₃ = 38). After diazotization (NaNO₂/HCl, 0-5°C), R-NH₂ → R-N₂⁺. Now the leaving group is N₂ — the BEST leaving group in all of organic chemistry. This seemingly counterintuitive reaction COMPLETELY inverts the reactivity: a substrate with a terrible LG (R-NH₂) becomes one with the BEST LG possible (R-N₂⁺). Net: diazotization is a method to replace the -NH₂ group with virtually any nucleophile (OH, CN, halide, azide, etc.) via the diazonium intermediate. The diazotization is a 5-step mechanism generating nitrosonium NO⁺ as the active electrophile. | Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺) are stable intermediates that can be used in coupling reactions like aromatic diazonium → Aliphatic diazonium salts (R-N₂⁺, where R is alkyl) are EXTREMELY UNSTABLE — they immediately lose N₂ to give a carbocation (R⁺) which then reacts with any nucleophile present. This is why diazotisation of aliphatic primary amines in water gives alcohol (R⁺ + H₂O → R-OH). This route ONLY works for aliphatic amines (R ≠ Ar); aromatic diazonium (Ar-N₂⁺) is resonance-stabilised and stable at 0–5°C, useful for azo coupling and Sandmeyer reactions.
edges.exception_trap: Students apply SN2 mechanism to diazonium hydrolysis; mechanism is SN1 or DN+AN (phenyl cation).

edges.conditions: HBr (1 equiv); low temperature (–40°C or 0°C); kinetic conditions. 1,2-addition product: kinetic control at low T. Mechanism: H⁺ adds to C-1 → allylic cation (stabilised by resonance C-2 and C-4) → Br⁻ adds to C-2 (1,2-product, closer resonance structure, faster, kinetic). Low T prevents equilibration. Product: 3-bromo-1-butene.

reagent_1.conditions: HBr (1 equiv); RT (25°C) or higher temperature; thermodynamic conditions. 1,4-addition: allylic cation intermediate (C-2 and C-4 resonance) → Br⁻ adds to C-4 (1,4-product, more substituted alkene, thermodynamic). Trans (E) product preferred (more stable). Higher T → thermodynamic product. Equilibriu
reagent_1.note: 1,4-addition of HBr to buta-1,3-diene; TCR (Thermodynamically Controlled Rxn); 25°C or room temperature (high temperature relative to -40°C); H+ to C1 → allylic cation → Br- attacks C4 (1,4 product); product: (E)-1-bromobut-2-ene (CH3-CH=CH-CH2Br); 1,4-product = thermodynamic product = more stable (internal alkene CH=CH retained); at HIGH TEMP, thermodynamic product (1,4) is major; rule: 1,2-addition = KCR (-40°C); 1,4-addition = TCR (25°C); SAME allylic cation intermediate for both

reagent_2.conditions: Br₂ (1 equiv); CCl₄; thermodynamic conditions (TCP = thermodynamic control product; RT or higher T for 1,4 product). Mechanism: Br₂ → Br⁺ (bromonium-like) → allylic cation → Br⁻ attacks C-4 (1,4-addition) → (E)-1,4-dibromobut-2-ene (trans-1,4 is thermodynamic). Low T/CCl₄ cold → 1,2-addition product
reagent_2.note: 1,4-addition of Br2 (1eq) to buta-1,3-diene; Br+ to C1 → OPEN allylic cation (NOT closed bromonium; allylic resonance breaks the tight bromonium bridge) → Br- attacks C4 ANTI to C1-Br → (E)-1,4-dibromobut-2-ene (TRANS) = ONLY ONE PRODUCT (no cis isomer); key: even in 1,4-addition, the anti-addition geometry is maintained → trans product exclusively; Peter Sykes reference; TS = extended s-trans allylic cation; Br- comes from outside (anti) → trans diaxial addition across C1 and C4; Br2 1,4-addition to 1,3-diene always gives TRANS (E) product

reagent_3.conditions: HBr (1 equiv); RT (25°C); thermodynamic control. Isoprene (2-methylbuta-1,3-diene) + HBr → allylic cation → 1,4-addition product preferred at RT. H⁺ adds to C-1 → allylic cation stabilised by methyl at C-2 → Br⁻ attacks C-4 → (E)-1-bromo-2-methyl-2-butene (trans-1,4). Methyl group at C-2 stabilises
reagent_3.note: IIT Adv 2016 type; HBr addition to 2-methylbuta-1,3-diene (isoprene, CH2=C(CH3)-CH=CH2); H+ adds to C1 (NOT C4); reason: H+ to C1 → 3° allylic cation [CH3-C+(CH3)-CH=CH2 ↔ CH3-C(CH3)=CH-CH2+] = 3°+allylic (more stable ✓); H+ to C4 would give 2°+allylic (less stable ✗); TCP at 25°C: Br- attacks C4 of the more stable 3°-allylic cation → product (E)-1-bromo-2-methylbut-2-ene (CH2Br-C(CH3)=CH-CH3, 1,4-addition); key: always add H+ to give MOST STABLE allylic carbocation (3° > 2° > 1°)

edges.conditions: Cu catalyst; 300°C; air/O₂; catalytic oxidative dehydrogenation. HOCH₂CH₂OH → OHC-CHO (glyoxal) + 2H₂O. Industrial. Alternatively: KMnO₄ (mild, partial oxidation stops at aldehyde before carboxylic acid if carefully controlled). Also: HIO₄ cleaves 1,2-diols → 2 HCHO (not glyoxal, since vicinal diol cleaved).

reagent_1.conditions: Conc H₃PO₄; Δ (200–300°C); dehydration. HOCH₂CH₂OH → H₂C=CHOH (vinyl alcohol, enol) + H₂O → tautomerism → CH₃CHO (acetaldehyde). High-temperature dehydration; acid-catalysed. Enol formed first (thermodynamically unfavourable) → rapid keto-enol tautomerism → acetaldehyde.
reagent_1.note: Stereochemistry: syn-diol from OsO₄ or cold KMnO₄; anti-diol from epoxide then H₂O/base. Specify workup conditions explicitly in answers.

reagent_2.conditions: KHSO₄ or conc H₂SO₄; Δ (strong heat, >200°C); double dehydration (loss of 2 H₂O). Step 1: dehydration at C-2 → enol → propenediol intermediate. Step 2: 2nd dehydration → acrolein (CH₂=CH-CHO, prop-2-enal). Pungent, lachrymatory aldehyde. Characteristic smell of burning fat (triglyceride pyrolysis →

edges.conditions: Red phosphorus + excess HI; heat (Δ, reflux); exhaustive dehalogenation. Step 1: HI (or PI₃) replaces OH → 1,2,3-triiodopropane. Step 2: excess HI → reduces C-I bonds → propane. Proof of glycerol structure (historically). HI: red P + I₂ → PI₃ → SN2 on primary OH. Complete reduction.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: PI₃ (excess, from P + I₂); heat; SN2 on primary OH. Glycerol + PI₃ → 1,2,3-triiodopropane → elimination (HI + alkali or Zn) → allyl iodide (or propene with excess HI). Phosphorus halides substitute OH with I via phosphite ester intermediate → SN2. Primary OH reacts faster than secondary.

edges.conditions: KMnO₄ (hot, acidic, excess); reflux. Hot acidic KMnO₄ cleaves vicinal diol → 2 HCOOH (formic acid from primary OH → HCOOH by overoxidation). Contrast: periodic acid (HIO₄) also cleaves 1,2-diols to aldehydes/ketones/HCHO (milder, no overoxidation). KMnO₄ overoxidises HCHO → HCOOH → CO₂ at extreme conditions.

reagent_1.conditions: Conc HNO₃ (oxidising acid); heat; vigorous conditions. HNO₃ oxidises ethylene glycol completely → oxalic acid (ethanedioic acid, HOOC-COOH). HNO₃ is strong oxidant in this context. Compare: glycerol + conc HNO₃/H₂SO₄ → nitroglycerin (esterification, not oxidation — different reaction).

Alternatively: NaOH/I₂ oxidises CH₂OH → then the adjacent PhCHOH → PhCO → iodoform from CH₃ group? The product benzoate + CHI₃ means: PhCO-CHI₃ interm

edges.conditions: Acetic acid (2 equiv) or Ac₂O (2 equiv carefully); RT to 60°C; no special catalyst. 1° OH groups (C-1, C-3) are less hindered than 2° OH (C-2) → preferential esterification at positions 1 and 3. Selective under mild conditions. Can also use enzymatic acylation for strict regioselectivity.
edges.notes: Archetype ['III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Conc HNO₃ + conc H₂SO₄ (mixed acid, 1:2 ratio by vol); 0°C (CRITICAL — below 5°C, above → explosive decomposition); O-nitration of all 3 OH groups → glyceryl trinitrate (nitroglycerin). H₂SO₄ acts as dehydrant/proton source for NO₂⁺ generation. Temperature strictly <5°C. Product: oily liquid, highly sensitive to shock and heat. Alfred Nobel stabilised with kieselguhr → dynamite.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'II'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: RT to mild heat; neat amine or amine in EtOH or THF; primary or secondary amine (RNH₂ or R₂NH) opens epoxide. Without acid catalyst: amine (better nucleophile than H₂O) attacks at less hindered C (SN2, anti addition) → β-amino alcohol. With acid: more substituted C attacked (SN1-like). Product: trans-β-amino alcohol. Used in synthesis of amino alcohols for asymmetric synthesis.
edges.notes: Stereochemical outcome critical: syn addition (mCPBA), then ring-opening gives anti-addition overall. Track face selectivity carefully.
edges.exception_description: Tertiary amines are resistant to all oxidation → Tertiary amines react with peracids (mCPBA, H2O2, O3) to give amine oxides R3N+→O-; they are inert to KMnO4 only
edges.exception_trap: Students generalise from KMnO4 inertness to all oxidants

edges.conditions: RT; aqueous acid (dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl, pH 2–4) or aqueous base (NaOH). Acid opening: H⁺ protonates O → ring strain drives SN1-like opening at more substituted C (Markovnikov) → H₂O attacks from back → anti-diol (trans). Base opening: HO⁻ attacks at less hindered C (SN2, anti addition) → anti-diol. Either way: anti addition of two OH groups across former double bond.

H2O (unlabeled, 16O) attacks from ANTI face (backside of the 18O bridge) → forms new 16C-O bond from water, releases 18C-O as 18OH group on the other C. Trans diaxial opening preferred for cyclohexene epoxides. Product: β-halohydrin (trans if from symmetrical alkene). HBr/HCl: X attacks at more substituted C under acidic conditions.

Product: trans (anti) vicinal dihalide from cyclic epoxide. Compare: diol from H₂O opening, amino alcohol from amine opening.
