edges.conditions: PCl₅; anhydrous; RT to mild heat. RCOOR' + PCl₅ → RCOCl (acyl chloride) + R'Cl + POCl₃. Both O-C bonds cleaved: acyl-O gives RCOCl, alkyl-O gives R'Cl. Useful for converting esters to acyl chlorides without going through the free acid. Clean for primary R'.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: DIBALH (diisobutylaluminium hydride, DIBAL-H); dry toluene or hexane; –78°C (very low T critical!); anhydrous. Selective reduction: ester → aldehyde (stops at aldehyde at –78°C because product aldehyde-Al complex is stable; warming → alcohol). Mechanism: H⁻ from DIBALH attacks ester C=O → tetrahedral intermediate (stable Al-O chelate at –78°C) → aqueous workup → aldehyde. At RT: ester → alcohol (2 equiv DIBALH or full reduction).
edges.notes: DIBALH (diisobutylaluminium hydride) reduces ester to ALDEHYDE (stops at aldehyde, does NOT over-reduce to alcohol); ethyl ethanoate + DIBALH → ethanal (acetaldehyde); DIBALH is the MOST PREFERRED reagent for ester → aldehyde conversion; also: nitrile + DIBALH → aldehyde (after hydrolysis); amide + DIBALH → aldehyde (good yield); acyl halide + DIBALH → aldehyde; key: DIBALH stops at aldehyde stage because the aluminum alkoxide product is stable at -78°C and does not allow further reduction until workup
edges.exception_description: DIBALH_over_reduces_ester_to_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Dibalh over reduces ester to alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.

edges.conditions: H₃O⁺ (dil H₂SO₄ or HCl); H₂O (excess); reflux. Acid hydrolysis (AAc2 mechanism, most common): H⁺ protonates C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → H₂O → hemiacetal-like → EtOH leaves → acetic acid. Reversible (Fischer esterification reverse). Drive forward by excess H₂O. Base: NaOH (irreversible saponification) → sodium acetate → acidify → acetic acid.
edges.notes: Ester acid hydrolysis: ethyl ethanoate (CH3COOC2H5, ethyl acetate) + H3O+/Δ (reversible equilibrium) → ethanoic acid (CH3COOH) + ethanol (C2H5OH); MECHANISM: acid-catalyzed nucleophilic acyl substitution (AAC2): H+ protonates C=O of ester → nucleophilic attack of H2O → tetrahedral intermediate → proton transfers → -OR (alcohol) leaves → carboxylic acid; REVERSIBILITY: acid hydrolysis of esters is REVERSIBLE (equilibrium); the reverse reaction = Fischer esterification (RCOOH + R'OH ⇌ RCOOR' + H2O); use excess H2O to drive toward hydrolysis; GENERAL: any ester (RCOOR') + H3O+/Δ → RCOOH + R'OH; acid hydrolysis: RCOOH produced (free acid); compare basic: R-COO- produced (carboxylate, requires acidification to get RCOOH); for ALL acid derivatives (anhydride, acyl halide, ester, amide) + H3O+ → RCOOH + corresponding leaving group (R'COOH, HX, R'OH, NH3)

reagents: BAc2, most common basic hydrolysis, IRREVERSIBLE
edges.conditions: NaOH (aqueous, 1–2 mol/L); reflux (80–100°C); BAc2 mechanism (base, acyl, bimolecular). Mechanism: OH⁻ attacks ester C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → breakdown → carboxylate + R'OH. Irreversible (carboxylate is resonance-stabilised, does not re-esterify under basic conditions). Used for soap manufacture (triglyceride + NaOH → soap + glycerol). ¹⁸O in NaOH ends up in carboxylate product (acyl-O cleavage).
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄ + MeOH; RT. AAc1 (acid, acyl, unimolecular acyl-O cleavage): H⁺ protonates C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → acylium R-CO⁺ → loses CO → t-Bu⁺ (very stable 3°) → MeOH → t-BuOMe + CO. Only works for pivalate (2,2-dimethylpropanoate) and other very hindered esters where acyl C is inaccessible to nucleophile. CO evolution is diagnostic. Normal esters go AAc2.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: Fries rearrangement: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid, >1 equiv); anhydrous; heat (Δ, 100–200°C); CS₂, PhNO₂ or nitrobenzene solvent; or neat. Low T (room to 100°C) → ortho product (thermodynamic control of intramolecular delivery). High T (>150°C) → para product. Photo-Fries (UV, RT) → predominantly ortho. AlCl₃ cleaves ester O-CO → acylium → intramolecular FC acylation of phenol ring.

reagent_1.conditions:
GRIGNARD + TERTIARY AMIDE → KETONE (UNIQUE SNth PRODUCT OF AMIDES):

AMIDE REACTIVITY: Tertiary amide (R-CO-NR2) is the LEAST REACTIVE acid derivative in SNth. NaCl, carboxylate, alkoxide, 2° amine, etc. CANNOT displace NR2 from the amide under normal conditions (NR2⁻/NHR⁻ is a very poor LG, pKa of
reagent_1.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

edges.notes: Rearrangement step: product carbon skeleton differs from substrate. Always verify whether migration occurs before assigning product structure. | Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Fries_AlCl3_gives_C_acylation_not_O_acylation → Fries rearrangement (phenyl ester + AlCl₃/heat) gives C-acylation (ketone at ring carbon) NOT further O-acylation (which would give another ester). AlCl₃ causes the acyl group to migrate from O to C. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | Fries_high_T_gives_ortho_not_para → Exception to the standard rule: Fries high t gives ortho not para. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Fries_rearrangement_high_temperature_gives_para_product → Exception to the standard rule: Fries rearrangement high temperature gives para product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Expecting mono-addition with esters

edges.conditions: Fries rearrangement: AlCl₃ (Lewis acid, excess); heat (Δ, >150°C); anhydrous nitrobenzene or neat. High temperature → para product (4'-hydroxyacetophenone, 4-hydroxyphenyl ethanone). Low temperature → ortho product. AlCl₃ cleaves ester O-CO → acetyl group migrates to ring at para (high T). Used in synthesis of hydroxyphenyl ketones (sunscreen components).

reagent_1.conditions: Fries rearrangement at low T (ortho product): AlCl₃; anhydrous; CS₂ or PhNO₂ solvent; RT to 100°C → ortho product (1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one = 2'-hydroxyacetophenone). High T (>150°C) → para product (4'-hydroxyacetophenone). Acyl group migrates from O to ring C-ortho via intramolecular Friedel-
reagent_1.note: Fries rearrangement: aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R) + AlCl3/Delta → hydroxy aryl ketone (acyl group migrates from O-ester to the aromatic ring; OH is exposed on ring); phenyl ethanoate (Ph-O-CO-CH3) + AlCl3/Delta → 1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one (2-hydroxyacetophenone, ortho) as MAJOR at HIGH TEMP; 4-hydroxyacetophenone (para) MAJOR at LOW TEMP; KEY TEMPERATURE RULE: LOW temp → PARA product (kinetic); HIGH temp → ORTHO product (thermodynamic, chelation stabilization); products: ortho = cyclic chelate favored thermodynamically; para = less steric, kinetically favored; RELATED: Gattermann-Koch also introduces CHO to ring (but without rearrangement)

edges.notes: Fries rearrangement; phenyl acetate (O-acyl) + AlCl3 → 4-hydroxyacetophenone (C-acyl, para major); O-to-C acyl migration; low T favors para product; contrast: without AlCl3, phenol+CH3COCl gives O-acylation (ester) only | Fries rearrangement: aryl ester (Ar-O-CO-R) + AlCl3/Delta → hydroxy aryl ketone (acyl group migrates from O-ester to the aromatic ring; OH is exposed on ring); phenyl ethanoate (Ph-O-CO-CH3) + AlCl3/Delta → 1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)ethan-1-one (2-hydroxyacetophenone, ortho) as MAJOR at HIGH TEMP; 4-hydroxyacetophenone (para) MAJOR at LOW TEMP; KEY TEMPERATURE RULE: LOW temp → PARA product (kinetic); HIGH temp → ORTHO product (thermodynamic, chelation stabilization); products: ortho = cyclic chelate favored thermodynamically; para = less steric, kinetically favored; RELATED: Gattermann-Koch also introduces CHO to ring (but without rearrangement)

Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | Fries_AlCl3_gives_C_acylation_not_O_acylation → Fries rearrangement (phenyl ester + AlCl₃/heat) gives C-acylation (ketone at ring carbon) NOT further O-acylation (which would give another ester). AlCl₃ causes the acyl group to migrate from O to C. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | Fries_high_T_gives_ortho_not_para → Exception to the standard rule: Fries high t gives ortho not para. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gattermann_Koch_with_AlCl3_alone_sufficient → Exception to the standard rule: Gattermann koch with alcl3 alone sufficient. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Fries_rearrangement_high_temperature_gives_para_product → Exception to the standard rule: Fries rearrangement high temperature gives para product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

reagents: conc HX (HI > HBr, acidic conditions, SN1 or SN2 depending on R)
edges.conditions: Conc HX (HI > HBr >> HCl) or excess H₂SO₄; reflux. Mechanism: H⁺ protonates ether O → oxonium ion → SN1 (3° or 2° with HI/HBr) or SN2 (1° ethers, X⁻ attacks) → alkyl halide + alcohol → further halogenation of alcohol. HI cleaves fastest (I⁻ best nucleophile). Aryl ethers (PhOR): Ar-O cleaved → PhOH + R-X (aryl C-O too strong for SN on ring). Excess HI: PhOH → PhI ultimately.

reagent_1.conditions: PCl₅; RT to mild heat; anhydrous. R-O-R' + PCl₅ → RCl + R'Cl + POCl₃. Both C-O bonds cleaved. Compare with HX cleavage (gives one alcohol + one halide initially). PCl₅ is more reactive than HX; both C-O bonds broken. Less commonly used; HX is the standard ether cleavage.
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: PCl₅; anhydrous; RT to gentle heat. R-O-R' + PCl₅ → R-Cl + R'-Cl + POCl₃. Both C-O bonds cleaved. Contrast: HI cleavage of ethers (one C-O at a time, gives R-OH then R-I with excess HI). PCl₅ gives both halides directly. Less selective; used mainly for specific halide syntheses.
reagent_2.note: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_3.conditions:
ETHER CLEAVAGE WITH HX:

STEP 1: Protonation of ether O → oxonium ion R-O⁺H-R' (fast)
STEP 2: C-O bond cleavage — MECHANISM DEPENDS ON SUBSTITUTION:

IF any C is 3° (tertiary): → SN1: the 3° C-O bond breaks → 3° C⁺ → X⁻ captures → 3° R-X. Other fragment = alcohol.
   - 3°/3°: SN1 at either 3° C (rac
reagent_3.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_4.conditions: No 3° groups in ether → SN2 pathway. Oxonium formed: (CH₃)₂CH-O⁺H-CH₃. Br⁻ attacks at CH₃ (1°, less hindered than isopropyl 2°) from BACKSIDE → CH₃-Br (bromomethane) + (CH₃)₂CH-OH (propan-2-ol). Selectivity: SN2 at 1° vs 2° → strongly favors 1° (less steric hindrance). Product: CH₃Br (IUPAC: bromome
reagent_4.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. edges.conditions: BF₃·Et₂O (Lewis acid catalyst); CO (1–3 atm); RT to mild heat; anhydrous. Mechanism: BF₃ activates ether O → oxocarbenium (R⁺-O-R') → CO insertion → acylium → ester product. Koch reaction variant. Uncommon in lab; industrial interest for carbonylation of ethers. BF₃ is essential Lewis acid.
edges.notes: Archetype ['I', 'III'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.conditions: O₂ (air); RT, slow process; radical chain, alpha-C selectivity. Radical initiator (trace): ether stored in air slowly forms alpha-hydroperoxide. Alpha C-H abstraction by RO₂• → alpha radical → O₂ → hydroperoxide. HAZARD: ether peroxides accumulate in stored diethyl ether/THF → explosive on evaporation or heating. Always check peroxide level before distilling aged ether. Remove peroxides: ferrous sulphate, copper mesh, alumina.

reagent_1.conditions: Cl₂/hν or Cl₂/NBS; RT; radical alpha-C-H substitution. Similar selectivity to alkane halogenation but alpha-C (adjacent to O) is most reactive (O lone pair stabilises radical → alpha selectivity). Product: alpha-halo ether (R-O-CHX-R'). Used as alkylating agents (alpha-halo ethers are reactive elect
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

edges.notes: Radical mechanism: initiation required (hν or ROOR). Chain process — omitting any propagation step loses marks in mechanism questions. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Thermal Claisen rearrangement: heat (Δ, 200–250°C); no catalyst required; neat or high-boiling solvent (diethylaniline). Mechanism: [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement (pericyclic, chair-like TS); allyl group migrates from O to C-2 (ortho) with inversion at γ-carbon (γ,γ'-connectivity). If ortho blocked → further Cope → para product (Claisen-Cope). Product: 2-allylphenol (ortho). Stereospecific (E-allyl ether → Z-allylic C at product).

reagent_1.conditions: ARYL ETHER CLEAVAGE RULE [IIT JEE 2010]: The Ar-O bond (Ph-O) is NEVER broken by HX because the phenyl ring's lone pair on O donates into the ring (resonance) → partial C=O double bond character → Ar-O bond is much stronger than ordinary C-O. ONLY the alkyl C-O bond is cleaved. Products: ALWAYS phen
reagent_1.note: Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry.

reagent_2.conditions: 18O ISOTOPE LABELING MECHANISTIC PROOF for aryl ether cleavage rule: Ph-[18O]-cyclopentyl + H3O+ → SN1 at cyclopentyl (secondary, benzylic-adjacent). Mechanism: H+ protonates [18O] → Ph-18O+(H)-cyclopentyl → cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (NOT Ph-O bond per aryl ether rule) → cyclopentyl+ C+ + Ph-[18O]
reagent_2.note: EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.

edges.notes: Rearrangement step: product carbon skeleton differs from substrate. Always verify whether migration occurs before assigning product structure. | Archetype ['I'] transformation. Verify conditions carefully; the reagent identity determines mechanism and hence product regiochemistry/stereochemistry. | EAS: product regiochemistry determined by existing substituent(s). Check for conflicting or cooperating directors before assigning major product.
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para). | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

edges.conditions: Step 1: –78°C to 0°C; dry ether (Et₂O) or THF; anhydrous; HCHO (formaldehyde, gas or paraformaldehyde) added to RMgX → addition. Step 2: Aqueous workup H₃O⁺ (NH₄Cl/H₂O); RT. Product: primary alcohol with one more C than Grignard (RMgX + HCHO → R-CH₂-OH). Strictly anhydrous throughout; moisture destroys Grignard (R-MgX + H₂O → R-H + Mg(OH)X).

reagent_1.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; anhydrous; RCHO (aldehyde) + R'MgX → addition to carbonyl. Step 2: Aqueous workup (NH₄Cl/H₂O or dilute H₂SO₄). Product: secondary alcohol (new C-C bond formed, new stereocentre = racemic mixture). Carbonyl carbon hybridisation changes sp²→sp³. Grignard is organome
reagent_1.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

reagent_2.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; anhydrous; R₂CO (ketone) + R''MgX → addition. Step 2: Aqueous workup. Product: tertiary alcohol (three different or same R groups). Steric hindrance of ketone can slow reaction (especially with aryl Grignard + hindered ketone). Use THF (higher bp, better solvation
reagent_2.note: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.

edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Expecting mono-addition with esters

edges.conditions: Step 1: 0°C to RT; dry Et₂O or THF; dry CO₂ gas (passed through Grignard solution or solid dry ice added) + RMgX → addition → MgX-carboxylate. Step 2: Aqueous workup (H₃O⁺). Product: RCOOH (one carbon more than Grignard). Mechanism: nucleophilic addition of R⁻ to electrophilic C of CO₂. Double addition prevented (carboxylate is much less reactive than CO₂ toward RMgX).
edges.notes: Strict anhydrous conditions required. Proton source (H₂O, ROH) destroys Grignard before reaction with substrate.
