Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Assuming all phenols are weakly acidic

edges.conditions: Stepwise nitration: conc HNO₃/H₂SO₄; increasing temperature (50°C for first NO₂, 70°C for second, 100°C for third). Methyl (toluene) is EDG ortho/para director → successive nitrations at ortho and para positions. Third nitration requires higher T (ring deactivated by NO₂ groups). Product: TNT (2,4,6-trinitrotoluene = 1-methyl-2,4,6-trinitrobenzene). Three equiv mixed acid.

reagent_1.conditions: Br₂ (2 equiv) + Fe (or FeBr₃ Lewis acid); anhydrous; mild heat. Nitrobenzene: NO₂ is meta-director/EWG → EAS at meta positions (C-3 and C-5, both meta to NO₂). Two Br² added sequentially → 1,3-dibromo-5-nitrobenzene (meta-deactivated ring, need excess Br₂ and Lewis acid, higher T than for activated
reagent_1.note: Double bromination of nitrobenzene; NO2 is meta-director → Br goes to C-3 and C-5 (both meta positions); with 2 equiv Br2/Fe both meta positions are brominated; product = 1,3-dibromo-5-nitrobenzene (= 3,5-dibromonitrobenzene); KEY: EWG like NO2 directs to BOTH meta positions with excess electrophile; no ortho/para bromination occurs

reagent_2.conditions: IUPAC_common_to_systematic_aromatic_names
reagent_2.note: toluene=methylbenzene; anisole=methoxybenzene; phenol=hydroxybenzene; aniline=benzenamine; cumene=(1-methylethyl)benzene=isopropylbenzene; styrene=ethenylbenzene=vinylbenzene; o-xylene=1,2-dimethylbenzene; m-xylene=1,3-dimethylbenzene; p-xylene=1,4-dimethylbenzene; o-cresol=2-methylphenol; m-cresol=3-methylphenol; p-cresol=4-methylphenol; mesitylene=1,3,5-trimethylbenzene; isooctane=2,2,4-trimethylpentane; acetone=propan-2-one (NOT IUPAC: crossed out in notes)

edges.notes: TNT synthesis: 3 successive nitrations; methyl (EDG) directs ortho/para → 1st: 2- and 4-nitrotoluene; 2nd: 2,4-dinitrotoluene; 3rd: 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT); each step requires more vigorous conditions (NO2 deactivates ring) | Double bromination of nitrobenzene; NO2 is meta-director → Br goes to C-3 and C-5 (both meta positions); with 2 equiv Br2/Fe both meta positions are brominated; product = 1,3-dibromo-5-nitrobenzene (= 3,5-dibromonitrobenzene); KEY: EWG like NO2 directs to BOTH meta positions with excess electrophile; no ortho/para bromination occurs | toluene=methylbenzene; anisole=methoxybenzene; phenol=hydroxybenzene; aniline=benzenamine; cumene=(1-methylethyl)benzene=isopropylbenzene; styrene=ethenylbenzene=vinylbenzene; o-xylene=1,2-dimethylbenzene; m-xylene=1,3-dimethylbenzene; p-xylene=1,4-dimethylbenzene; o-cresol=2-methylphenol; m-cresol=3-methylphenol; p-cresol=4-methylphenol; mesitylene=1,3,5-trimethylbenzene; isooctane=2,2,4-trimethylpentane; acetone=propan-2-one (NOT IUPAC: crossed out in notes)
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para). | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

edges.conditions: CHCl₃ + excess alc. KOH; heat; primary amines ONLY
edges.notes: SCOPE RESTRICTION: primary amines ONLY. Secondary and tertiary amines give NO reaction. This is the carbylamine test — the isocyanide has a characteristically foul smell. Mechanism: CHCl₃ + KOH → :CCl₂ (dichlorocarbene) → reacts with R-NH₂. EXCEPTION: aniline (ArNH₂) also gives positive (it is a primary amine). JEE TRAP: 2° and 3° amines do not give isocyanide — this test distinguishes 1° from 2°/3°. qualitative_test_ref: carbylamine
edges.exception_description: Amines are more basic than aniline → Diphenylamine (pKb~13.2) is less basic than aniline (pKb~9.4) due to greater lone pair delocalisation over two rings | Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | Reduction of ArNO2 always gives ArNH2 → Zn/NH4Cl/H2O (neutral conditions) gives phenylhydroxylamine (ArNHOH), not aniline | pyrrole N lone pair available for protonation like aliphatic amines → Pyrrole N lone pair is in a p-orbital, part of the 6πe⁻ aromatic ring system. Protonating pyrrole N removes 2πe⁻ from the aromatic system → loses aromaticity. This is highly unfavorable → pyrrole is an extremely WEAK base (pKa ~ -3.8). Pyrrole prefers C-2 protonation (losing less resonance energy). Contrast: aniline pKaH ~4.6 (N lone pair NOT in aromatic ring after protonation, because NH₂ lone pair participates via resonance but ring stays aromatic when N is protonated). | o-CH3 aniline is LESS basic than unsubstituted aniline despite CH3 being electron-donating → o-Methylaniline (2-methylaniline, o-toluidine) Kb = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ < aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰ — o-CH₃ DECREASES aniline basicity. CH₃ at para (p-toluidine, 4-methylaniline) Kb = 12 × 10⁻¹⁰ — p-CH₃ INCREASES basicity. So the +M/+I of CH₃ would predict increased basicity at ALL positions, but at ortho the STERIC INHIBITION OF PROTONATION (SIP) effect overrides: the ortho methyl sterically blocks H₃O⁺ approach to the N lone pair → decreases Kb. BOXED: p > m > aniline > o for methylaniline basicity. This is the SIP mechanism — identical in spirit to SIR for carboxylic acids. | p-NO2 aniline less basic than m-NO2 aniline (para more destructive to aniline basicity than meta) → p-Nitroaniline (4-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰ < m-Nitroaniline (3-nitroaniline) Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰ — para-NO₂ DESTROYS aniline basicity more than meta-NO₂. The through-ring resonance (N lone pair → ring → p-NO₂) directly withdraws electron density from N when NO₂ is at para: the nitrogen lone pair is in direct resonance with the ring → p-NO₂ can pull it through the ring continuously. At meta: NO₂ only has -I (no through-ring resonance path to N), less destructive. BASICITY order: aniline >> m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ > o-NO₂ (o is worst due to both -I proximity + -M field). | pyrrole N is LESS basic than aniline N even though both are aromatic amines → Pyrrole (1H-pyrrole, pKa of conjugate acid ≈ -3.8) is LESS BASIC than aniline (pKa of conjugate acid ≈ 4.6) by about 8 pKa units (10⁸× less basic). Both are aromatic amines, but: aniline N lone pair is in resonance with the ring (resonance weakens basicity) but the N still provides only partial lone pair donation into the ring. Pyrrole N lone pair IS the 2nd pair of aromatic π electrons that make the ring 6π aromatic — removing the lone pair from ring participation DESTROYS aromaticity. Therefore, pyrrole resists protonation far more than aniline. Nreso (aniline) >> Nano (pyrrole) in basicity. | N,N-dialkyl aniline is more basic than aniline despite both having N in resonance with ring → N,N-Dimethylaniline (PhN(CH₃)₂) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), not due to +I of CH₃ alone, but primarily due to SIR (Steric Inhibition of Resonance). In aniline, the N lone pair delocalizes freely into the ring (N: → ring, +M). In N,N-dimethylaniline, the two methyl groups on N sterically hinder the N-ring bond from achieving optimal planarity for lone pair conjugation → less N→ring resonance → more lone pair available at N → more basic. This is the SIR effect operating at the N-substituent level (not ring substituent). Additionally, +I of CH₃ also helps. But the SIR component is the primary mechanistic reason. | o-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is MORE basic than aniline — exception to ortho effect rule → 2-Aminophenol (o-OH-aniline) is MORE BASIC than aniline (PhNH₂), opposite to all other ortho-substituted anilines. Rule ⑦ states all ortho substituents decrease aniline basicity (SIP effect), but OH at ortho is the SOLE EXCEPTION. Reason: the o-OH forms an intramolecular H-bond with the protonated NH₃⁺ group in the conjugate acid: (-NH₃⁺·····:O-H, where OH oxygen donates H-bond to N⁺H₃) → stabilizes the anilinium ion → more stable conjugate acid → more basic amine. This is an intramolecular solvation of the conjugate acid that MORE than compensates for the SIP effect. So for aniline: o-OH aniline > aniline > o-CH₃, o-OCH₃, o-NO₂, o-Cl, o-Br, o-NH₂ anilines. | 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-aminoaniline) is LESS basic than aniline despite NH2 being a +M group at ortho → 1,2-Phenylenediamine (benzene-1,2-diamine, o-NH₂-aniline) has Kb = 3 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline Kb = 4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰. Despite the NH₂ group being a strong +M group (which at para position would dramatically increase basicity: p-NH₂-aniline Kb = 140 × 10⁻¹⁰), at the ORTHO position the SIP effect overwhelms the +M benefit → net basicity DECREASES below aniline. The o-NH₂ group creates steric inhibition of protonation, reducing solvation of the anilinium ion. This is why the NH₂ order for aniline is: p(140) > m(10) > PhNH₂(4.2) > o(3) — with ortho LESS basic than aniline itself. | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | m-methoxybaniline is LESS basic than aniline and LESS basic than o-methoxyaniline → m-Methoxyaniline (3-methoxyaniline) has Kb = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰, which is LESS than aniline (4.2 × 10⁻¹⁰) AND less than o-methoxyaniline (3 × 10⁻¹⁰). OCH₃ is a +M group, so students expect all OCH₃-anilines more basic than aniline. But at meta, +M effect of OCH₃ does NOT reach the NH₂ nitrogen (meta is NOT activated by +M through resonance) → only the -I effect of OCH₃ operates at meta → slight reduction in basicity. At ortho, OCH₃ has SIP (reduces basicity) BUT also some field-effect +M partially compensates → o-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=3) slightly MORE basic than m-OCH₃-aniline (Kb=2). ORDER: p-OCH₃(20) > aniline(4.2) > o-OCH₃(3) > m-OCH₃(2). | NaNH2 (sodium amide, NaNH2/NH3(l)) in benzyne reactions acts only as the nucleophile (NH2- attacks the benzene ring directly) in a normal SNAr mechanism, similar to NaOMe acting as nucleophile in addition-elimination SNAr → NaNH₂ in benzyne reactions acts as BOTH the BASE (not the nucleophile, in the first step) AND the NUCLEOPHILE (in the second step). Step 1: NH₂⁻ acts as BASE to remove H ortho to C-Cl (strong enough base: pKa of NH₃ ≈ 38, so NH₂⁻ can easily deprotonate aryl C-H adjacent to Cl). This forms a carbanion, which then expels Cl⁻ → benzyne. Step 2: NH₂⁻ (same or another molecule) adds to the reactive benzyne as NUCLEOPHILE → aryl amide → protonated → aniline. The product is ANILINE (Ar-NH₂), same Nu as in the reagent. If the question asks 'how many moles of NaNH₂ are needed?': theoretically 1 mol (1 as base, then the same NH₂⁻ or another can be the nucleophile — but practically often 2 mol used in excess). The key distinction from NaOMe: NaOMe cannot act as a base to form benzyne (it's not strong enough for ortho deprotonation from an unactivated ring) → NaOMe proceeds via Meisenheimer only. NaNH₂ is a stronger base → can form benzyne. | 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene reacts with NaNH2 in liquid ammonia to give the expected aniline derivative (2,6-dimethylaniline) via the benzyne mechanism, since NaNH2 is the standard reagent for benzyne reactions → 2,6-Dimethylchlorobenzene does NOT react with NaNH₂ in NH₃(l) — NO REACTION. Reason: the benzyne mechanism requires an H at the ortho position to Cl (for NH₂⁻ to deprotonate → form benzyne). In 2,6-dimethylchlorobenzene, BOTH ortho positions to Cl (C2 and C6) are occupied by CH₃ groups (NO ortho-H adjacent to Cl) → NH₂⁻ cannot find an ortho-H to remove → benzyne CANNOT form → no reaction. Also: no -M EWG on the ring → Meisenheimer SNAr also impossible. The substrate is completely inert toward NaNH₂. This is the STERIC BLOCKING of benzyne formation. | -NH2 group on benzene is activating EDG (+M dominates over -I) → When aniline is treated with acid, it becomes phenylammonium (Ph-NH3⁺). The +ve charged N has NO lone pair for resonance → complete reversal from +M EDG to pure -I EWG. Phenylammonium is a META-DIRECTOR and ring DEACTIVATOR. EAS on aniline·HCl gives predominantly meta product — opposite to free aniline (ortho/para).
edges.exception_trap: Assuming more nitrogen = more basic in polyaromatic amines

edges.conditions: acid hydrolysis; reflux; R-NC + H₂O → R-NH₂ + HCOOH (formic acid)
edges.notes: Isocyanide hydrolysis: R-NC + H₂O → R-NH₂ + HCOOH. Gives primary amine + formic acid. Useful in synthesis to purify primary amines (form isocyanide, purify by smell/bp, then hydrolyze back).

edges.conditions: Autoxidation: O2/hv on benzylic or allylic C-H bonds adjacent to halide. Not a standard JEE named reaction. Radical chain: R-CH2-X + O2 → R-CHX-OO· → eventually → R-COOH (via hydroperoxide). REFERENCE ONLY — not a primary JEE transformation.
edges.notes: Corrected from incorrect acid_chloride product. O2/hv gives autoxidation (radical mechanism) on activated positions → carboxylic acid via hydroperoxide. NOT a standard JEE reaction. Marked for awareness only.
edges.exception_description: HX adds Markovnikov to alkene → HBr adds anti-Markovnikov in presence of peroxides (radical chain) | hydrazine (NH2-NH2) is MORE nucleophilic than ammonia (NH3) despite being a weaker base → Hydrazine (diazane, NH₂-NH₂) is a BETTER nucleophile than ammonia (NH₃) despite being a WEAKER base (pKa NH₂NH₃⁺ ≈ 8.1 vs pKa NH₄⁺ = 9.25 → hydrazine is less basic). This is the ALPHA-EFFECT: the N adjacent to the nucleophilic N in hydrazine has lone pairs that REPEL the lone pair on the nucleophilic N → raises the energy of N's HOMO → faster attack on electrophilic center. Similarly: HOO⁻ (hydroperoxide) > OH⁻ despite peroxide being a weaker base. The alpha-effect always INCREASES nucleophilicity above what basicity alone would predict. | Hydrogen peroxide (dihydrogen dioxide, H2O2) is a weak acid and stable in ground state → Under UV irradiation, H2O2 undergoes homolytic O-O bond fission to give 2 HO• (hydroxyl radicals) — among the most reactive radicals in chemistry, initiating chain reactions even at room temperature with UV exposure
edges.exception_trap: HCl and HI do NOT show peroxide effect

edges.conditions: Lithium-halogen exchange: R-X + n-BuLi → R-Li (organolithium/carbanion) + n-Bu-X. Requires aryl or vinyl halides (or activated alkyl halides). Very rapid at -78°C in THF. R-Li behaves as carbanion nucleophile.
edges.notes: Corrected from incorrect gem-dihalide product. n-BuLi/RLi + R-X → R-Li (organolithium) via Li-halogen exchange. Product is a carbanion/organolithium. Used to make reactive organolithium nucleophiles. Distinct from Grignard formation (Mg/ether).
edges.exception_description: Grignard reagent adds to carbonyl once → Grignard with ester adds twice (first to give ketone intermediate, then again to give tertiary alcohol) | When Grignard reagent RMgX reacts with an ester R'COOR, the product is a ketone R'-CO-R because the Grignard acts as R- nucleophile in SNth, displacing OR- leaving group to give ketone, and the reaction stops at the ketone stage → Grignard + ESTER gives TERTIARY ALCOHOL (not ketone), because the initially formed ketone R'-CO-R is MORE REACTIVE than the ester (acyl halide > ketone vs. ester > ketone in reactivity; ketone has no LG = not SNth, but it IS reactive toward Grignard by addition). The KETONE intermediate reacts IMMEDIATELY with the second equivalent of Grignard → R'-C(-OMgX)(-R)(-R) → acid workup → R'-C(OH)R2 (tertiary alcohol). Thus ester + 2 RMgX → tertiary alcohol (overall). To isolate the ketone from ester + Grignard: CANNOT be done under normal conditions. Exception to the exception: AMIDE + RMgX → KETONE (the amine LG only departs on acid workup, and the tetrahedral intermediate with the amine is STABLE until acidified → the ketone is NOT formed in situ during the reaction → it doesn't react further with Grignard) → can isolate ketone from amide + Grignard. | nitrile_only_one_Grignard_gives_ketone_not_tertiary_alcohol → Exception to the standard rule: Nitrile only one grignard gives ketone not tertiary alcohol. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | HCN_Grignard_acid_base_not_nucleophilic_addition → Exception to the standard rule: Hcn grignard acid base not nucleophilic addition. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_CO2_gives_ketone_two_additions → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard co2 gives ketone two additions. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | alkyl_fluoride_reacts_with_Mg_to_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Alkyl fluoride reacts with mg to form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | aryl_and_vinyl_halides_do_not_form_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Aryl and vinyl halides do not form grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | secondary_amines_dont_react_with_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Secondary amines dont react with grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Grignard_SN2_works_with_all_alkyl_halides_including_tertiary → Exception to the standard rule: Grignard sn2 works with all alkyl halides including tertiary. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Gilman_reagent_R2CuLi_reacts_with_ketones_like_Grignard → Exception to the standard rule: Gilman reagent r2culi reacts with ketones like grignard. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
edges.exception_trap: Expecting mono-addition with esters

edges.conditions: Iodoform-type cleavage of methyl esters: CH3-CO-OR + I2/NaOH → CHI3 (iodoform) + RCOO⁻ + ROH. Restricted to methyl esters (CH3COO-R). Alkaline hydrolysis (saponification) competes. After acidification, carboxylic acid RCOOH is the organic product.
edges.notes: Corrected from incorrect alkyl_halide product. Only methyl esters react with I2/NaOH via iodoform mechanism; general esters undergo saponification (aq NaOH) → RCOO⁻ + ROH. Product after acidification is carboxylic acid.
edges.exception_description: Positive iodoform test for all CH3CO- containing compounds → CH3COOH and ALL acid derivatives (ester, anhydride, acid chloride, amide) are NEGATIVE for iodoform despite having CH3CO group | Cyclohexanone is iodoform negative (no CH3CO group) → Cyclohexanone DOES give CHI3 via ring-opening; triiodination at alpha-C then base cleaves ring → CHI3 + sodium adipate (dicarboxylate) | EtOAc (ethyl acetate) is haloform negative (it's an ester) → EtOAc gives INDIRECT positive iodoform: OH⁻ saponifies EtOAc → EtOH → EtOH gives iodoform (HCOO⁻ + CHI3) | Methanol gives iodoform (it has C-H bond alpha to OH) → MeOH gives NEGATIVE iodoform test; it oxidizes to HCHO (formaldehyde) which has NO alpha-CH3 group → formate only
edges.exception_trap: Students confuse functional group presence with reactivity; acid derivatives cannot form enolate under basic iodoform conditions

edges.conditions: Formaldehyde (HCHO, an aldehyde) + phenol → condensation polymer. H⁺ catalyst + phenol excess: Novolac (linear, thermoplastic). OH⁻ + HCHO excess: Resole → Bakelite (cross-linked, thermoset). Reaction: electrophilic aromatic substitution at o/p positions of phenol ring.
edges.notes: Corrected from incorrect phenol as product. HCHO (formaldehyde) is the aldehyde SM; phenol is the co-reactant; polymer (Bakelite/Novolac) is the product. Phenol is NOT produced from this reaction — it is consumed.
edges.exception_description: Halogenation of benzene requires Lewis acid → Phenol and aniline react with Br₂/H₂O without any catalyst due to strong ring activation; product is tribrominated | All acids react with Na₂CO₃ to give CO₂ → Phenol (pKa ~10) does NOT react with Na₂CO₃ — no CO₂ evolved. NaHCO₃ also fails. | Benzene ring halogenation requires Lewis acid catalyst (FeBr₃/AlCl₃) → Phenol undergoes 2,4,6-tribromination with Br₂/H₂O with NO catalyst — instantaneous white precipitate. | Acyl/ester group migrates from carbon to carbon in 1,2-shifts (Ar > R > H) → In dienone-phenol rearrangement, -COOEt migrates BETTER than R, which migrates better than Ar (order: -COO⁻ > R > Ar). | Bromination with Br₂/non-polar solvent gives same ratio as aqueous bromination → Phenol + Br₂/CS₂ → para-monobromination only. Phenol + Br₂/H₂O → 2,4,6-tribromination. Solvent controls degree of substitution. | All phenols do not react with NaHCO₃ → 2,4-dinitrophenol (and some other highly electron-deficient phenols) DO react with NaHCO₃ — they are acidic enough to displace CO₂ | CAN test: all alcohols give red color → 3° alcohol gives brown/green (not red) with CAN; phenol also gives brown/green | para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol despite NO2 being closer at ortho → 4-Nitrophenol (para-nitrophenol) Ka > 2-nitrophenol (ortho-nitrophenol). Despite NO₂ being closer to OH at ortho (stronger inductive + field), o-nitrophenol is LESS acidic than p-nitrophenol. Reason: 2-nitrophenol forms a 6-membered intramolecular H-bond (O-H···O=N-O ring) in the NEUTRAL form, stabilizing the acid → makes it less willing to ionize → LOWER Ka. The p-nitrophenol has no geometric possibility for intramolecular H-bond → full -M and -I stabilization of p-nitrophenoxide (4-nitrophenoxide) → higher Ka. Same pattern for o-COOH phenol vs p-COOH phenol. | F is least acidifying halogen at para position despite being most electronegative → At the para position on benzoic acid or phenol, F (fluorine, most electronegative halogen) is the LEAST EFFECTIVE at increasing acidity. F has the strongest +M (mesomeric donation via 2p-2p lone pair overlap) among halogens, which partially cancels its -I effect at para. I (iodine, least electronegative halogen) has the WEAKEST +M (5p-2p overlap, poor) → only -I operates → I is most acidifying at para for phenol. Quantitative: for phenols, pKa(p): F=9.95, Cl=9.38, Br=9.34, I=9.10. Para acidity order: I > Br > Cl > F. This is the OPPOSITE of what electronegativity would predict. | ortho-aminophenol (2-aminophenol) is more acidic than phenol despite NH2 being strong +M donor → 2-Aminophenol (o-aminophenol) is MORE ACIDIC than phenol (pKa of PhOH ≈ 10; pKa of 2-aminophenol slightly lower). This seems contradictory since NH₂ is a strong +M donor (which would destabilize PhO⁻ and decrease acidity). Reason: in the 2-aminophenoxide anion (o-H₂N-C₆H₄-O⁻), the amino group (-NH₂) forms an INTRAMOLECULAR HYDROGEN BOND with the alkoxide (-O⁻) in a 5-membered ring: N-H···O⁻. This H-bond stabilizes the anion more than the +M of NH₂ destabilizes it → net increase in acidity. At para: no such H-bond possible → +M of p-NH₂ purely destabilizes PhO⁻ → p-aminophenol LESS acidic than phenol. Order: o-NH₂ > m-NH₂ > phenol > p-NH₂. | NaHCO3 does NOT react with regular phenol (only with carboxylic acids and stronger acids) → Phenol (PhOH, pKa=10) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (conjugate acid = H₂CO₃, pKa=6.35). For the reaction to work, phenol must be a STRONGER acid than H₂CO₃. Since H₂CO₃ pKa=6.35 < phenol pKa=10 → H₂CO₃ is MORE ACIDIC than phenol → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate phenol. Only compounds with pKa < ~6 react with NaHCO₃ (give CO₂ gas). Exception: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4) DOES react with NaHCO₃ (it's more acidic than H₂CO₃). Regular phenol: reacts with NaOH but NOT with NaHCO₃. This is a standard JEE test for identifying carboxylic acids vs phenols. | aliphatic alcohol + NaOH gives no reaction (alcohol less acidic than water) → R-OH + NaOH → X (no reaction). Aliphatic alcohols (pKa 16-18) do NOT react with NaOH because they are LESS ACIDIC than water (H₂O pKa = 15.7). The equilibrium R-OH + NaOH ⇌ RO⁻Na⁺ + H₂O lies FAR to the LEFT. Contrast with phenol (pKa=10) which DOES react with NaOH (phenol more acidic than water). Students who know 'Na reacts with alcohol' assume NaOH also reacts — it does not. Sodium METAL reacts with R-OH (produces H₂ gas, product Na⁺RO⁻, because H₂ pKa=35 >> R-OH pKa 16-18 → alcohol is much more acidic than H₂). | p-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 despite being one of the more acidic phenols → p-Nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃ (aqueous sodium hydrogencarbonate). Even though p-nitrophenol is far more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), its pKa (7.2) is still GREATER than H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → p-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → NaHCO₃ cannot deprotonate it. The NaHCO₃ test distinguishes RCOOH from ANY PHENOL (including activated nitrophenols): only RCOOH (pKa<6) gives CO₂ gas with NaHCO₃. Exceptions that DO react: 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, pKa≈0.4), 2,4-dinitrophenol (pKa≈4.1), squaric acid (pKa≈1.5). | m-nitroaniline is MORE basic than p-nitroaniline despite both having NO2 withdrawing group → m-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.029 × 10⁻¹⁰; p-Nitroaniline has Kb = 0.001 × 10⁻¹⁰. Meta-nitroaniline is 29 TIMES more basic than para-nitroaniline, even though NO₂ is an electron-withdrawing group at both positions. Reason: at PARA position, NO₂ exerts BOTH -I AND -M effects (directly delocalized from para position to the NH₂ nitrogen via ring resonance: N:→ring→NO₂) → strongly reduces N lone pair → MUCH less basic. At META position, NO₂ exerts only -I effect (no -M at meta) → less deactivation → more basic. This is the OPPOSITE of what occurs in ACIDITY of phenols: for phenol, p-NO₂-phenol is MORE ACIDIC than m-NO₂-phenol (same logic: -M at para stabilizes phenoxide better). For aniline BASICITY: m-NO₂ > p-NO₂ (less deactivated at meta). | 2,4-dinitrophenol reacts with NaHCO3 but o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol do NOT → 2,4-Dinitrophenol (pKa ≈ 4.1) REACTS with NaHCO₃ (CO₂ evolved), but o-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) and p-nitrophenol (pKa ≈ 7.2) do NOT react with NaHCO₃. The threshold is H₂CO₃ pKa₁ = 6.35. 2,4-dinitrophenol has pKa < 6.35 (more acidic than H₂CO₃) → reacts. Mono-nitrophenols (pKa ≈ 7.2) have pKa > 6.35 → less acidic than H₂CO₃ → do NOT react. This means: even though o-NO₂-phenol is more acidic than plain phenol (pKa=10), it STILL does not react with NaHCO₃ (7.2 > 6.35). Only adding a SECOND NO₂ group brings the pKa below the H₂CO₃ threshold. Students who think 'any activated phenol reacts with NaHCO₃' are wrong. | o-nitrophenol does NOT react with NaHCO3 even though it has an electron-withdrawing NO2 group → o-Nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol, pKa ≈ 7.2) does NOT react with NaHCO₃, despite the NO₂ group at ortho position being a strong electron-withdrawing group that makes the phenol more acidic. The pKa (7.2) still exceeds H₂CO₃ pKa₁ (6.35) → o-nitrophenol is less acidic than H₂CO₃ → no NaHCO₃ reaction. Interestingly, o-NO₂ phenol actually has a LOWER pKa than p-NO₂ phenol might suggest due to intramolecular H-bonding (o-NO₂ reduces ionization somewhat because the intramolecular H-bond in the un-ionized form stabilizes the phenol itself), but both o and p mono-nitrophenols fall above the 6.35 threshold. Both require NaOH (not NaHCO₃) for deprotonation. | phenoxide PhO⁻ is a BAD leaving group despite being resonance-stabilized into an aromatic ring → Phenoxide (C₆H₅O⁻) is a POOR leaving group despite the negative charge being resonance-delocalized into the benzene ring. Student expects resonance-stabilization → stable anion → good LG. WRONG: phenol pKa ≈ 9.99 → phenoxide is a relatively STRONG base (much stronger than acetate pKa 4.76) → poor LG. CONSEQUENCES: Aryl ethers (Ar-O-R) cannot be cleaved by SN2 because phenoxide is too poor a LG. Diaryl ethers (Ar-O-Ar) are essentially inert to nucleophilic substitution. Compare: alcohol (OH, pKa 15.7) = terrible LG; phenol (pKa 9.99) = bad LG; acetic acid (pKa 4.76) = mediocre LG; sulfonic acid (pKa <0) = excellent LG. | Phenyl tert-butyl ether (Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃) + HI gives iodobenzene (Ph-I) + tert-butanol because I⁻ attacks the phenyl ring (which appears to be the electrophilic site) or the reaction follows SN2 on the least hindered C → Ph-O-C(CH₃)₃ + HI gives PHENOL (Ph-OH) + (CH₃)₃CI (tert-butyl iodide). The Ar-O (Ph-O) bond NEVER breaks → Ph-I is NOT formed. The tBu-O bond breaks via SN1 (tBu⁺ forms as 3° carbocation → I⁻ captures) → (CH₃)₃CI. Ph gets the OH (from H⁺ + Ph-O⁻ → Ph-OH). The tBu group gets the I. This is the opposite of what naive application of 'SN2 at less hindered C' would predict (that would give SN2 at the 3 methyls of tBu, giving CH₃I not (CH₃)₃CI). Instead: SN1 operates (3° group → C⁺ forms → I⁻) → gives the 3° iodide. | Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) reacts with concentrated HI to give iodobenzene (Ph-I) and phenol (Ph-OH), similar to how aryl alkyl ethers react → Diphenyl ether (Ph-O-Ph) does NOT react with HI (or any HX) under ordinary conditions. x = 0 (zero equivalents consumed). REASON: Both C-O bonds in Ph-O-Ph are Ar-O bonds (aryl-O). The general rule 'in aryl ether, Ar-O never breaks — only alkyl C-O breaks' applies to BOTH sides in diphenyl ether. Since there is NO alkyl C-O bond (both sides are Ph), there is nothing for I⁻ to attack via SN2 or SN1. Net result: no reaction. This is distinct from Ph-O-R (aryl ALKYL ether) where the alkyl side can react. | Benzofuran (aromatic 5-membered ring containing O fused to benzene) reacts with HI because it contains an oxygen that can be protonated and the C-O bonds can be cleaved → Benzofuran does NOT react with HI (x=0). Despite having an O atom that can in principle be protonated, benzofuran is an AROMATIC COMPOUND (10 π electrons total, both rings aromatic). The O in benzofuran donates its lone pair into the ring (part of the 10π aromatic system). The two C-O bonds in benzofuran (C2-O and C8a-O in numbering) are both part of the aromatic ring → Ar-O type bonds embedded in the aromatic system → cannot be cleaved by HX. Contrast with 2,3-DIHYDROBENZOFURAN (non-aromatic, the furan ring is saturated): it HAS aliphatic C-O bonds (C2-O and C3-O are sp3) → those CAN be cleaved by HI → gives 2-(2-iodoethyl)phenol type product. Aromaticity = protection from HX cleavage. | 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxy benzene, like found in safrole and piperine) does not react with HI because it has Ar-O bonds similar to phenyl ethers → 1,3-Benzodioxole DOES react with HI (x=2, 2 equivalents needed). Although the oxygens are both attached to the aromatic ring (the Ar-O bonds are indeed present), the critical reaction site is the CH₂ GROUP of the methylenedioxy bridge (-O-CH₂-O-). The CH₂ between the two oxygens is ALIPHATIC (sp3 carbon) and has TWO O-CH₂ bonds (not Ar-CH₂ bonds) → I⁻ CAN perform SN2 at this CH₂. So: 2 HI + 1,3-benzodioxole → catechol (1,2-benzenediol, both phenolic OH groups freed) + CH₂I₂ (diiodomethane). The Ar-O bonds don't break — catechol's O groups were the Ar-O bonds, and they are preserved as phenol OH groups. What breaks: the two O-CH₂ bonds (the aliphatic bonds to the methylene carbon). x=2 (not 0!) | In the 18O isotope labeling experiment for Ph-18O-cyclopentyl + H3O+, the labeled oxygen (18O) would end up in cyclopentanol because the nucleophile (H2O) attacks at the oxygen position → The 18O label ends up in PHENOL, NOT in cyclopentanol. REASON: In aryl ether hydrolysis, the cyclopentyl C-O bond breaks (alkyl C-O, not Ar-O). The 18O stays attached to phenyl (Ph-18O- = phenoxide with label → Ph-18OH = labeled phenol). The WATER (unlabeled 16O) provides a NEW oxygen to cyclopentanol: H2-16O attacks cyclopentyl+ → cyclopentyl-16OH (unlabeled). Net: labeled O in phenol, unlabeled O in cyclopentanol. If Ph-O bond had broken instead (wrong mechanism): 18O would be in cyclopentanol (wrong labeling pattern). The experimental observation (18O in phenol) PROVES the mechanism (alkyl C-O breaks, Ar-O intact). | Chlorobenzene cannot undergo SNAr because it has no electron-withdrawing group (EWG) to stabilize the Meisenheimer complex; the reaction simply fails → Chlorobenzene CAN undergo SNAr via the BENZYNE MECHANISM (not Meisenheimer) under harsh conditions (623K, 300 atm). This is the Dow Process for phenol synthesis. The benzyne mechanism bypasses the Meisenheimer requirement entirely: instead of Nu adding to C bearing L (as in addition-elimination), a BASE removes the ortho-H and L⁻ departs to form BENZYNE (1,2-didehydrobenzene). Then Nu adds to the reactive benzyne. No -M group is needed because the Meisenheimer intermediate is never formed. Requirements: strong base (NaOH, NaNH₂), high temperature and pressure for unactivated rings (or NaNH₂/NH₃(l) for laboratory scale). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | aspirin_phenol_OH_esterified_COOH_stays_free → In aspirin synthesis (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride → aspirin), the phenol OH is esterified (acetylated) while the carboxylic acid COOH remains free. | ZnCl2_weakest_Lewis_acid_not_FC → ZnCl₂ is a weaker Lewis acid than AlCl₃, FeCl₃, or BF₃ and is NOT suitable as a Friedel-Crafts catalyst for reactions with unreactive arene substrates. It is used only with very reactive substrates (phenol, furan) or in the Lucas test. | picric_acid_direct_HNO3_phenol_low_yield → Exception to the standard rule: Picric acid direct hno3 phenol low yield. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply. | Phenol has an -OH group so it should give positive CAN test like other alcohols → Phenol gives NEGATIVE CAN test. The CAN reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆] forms a characteristic RED complex with aliphatic alcohols (1° and 2°) because the -OH coordinates to Ce⁴⁺. Phenol does NOT form this complex under standard CAN conditions, giving a negative result. 3° alcohols give brown/green (not red) due to absence of α-H for Ce⁴⁺ oxidation. | All nitrophenols are more acidic than simple phenol, so all should react with NaHCO₃ → Mono-nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol pKa ~7.2, 4-nitrophenol pKa ~7.1) are more acidic than phenol (pKa ~10) but STILL NOT ACIDIC ENOUGH to react with NaHCO₃ (which can neutralise acids with pKa < 6.3). Only when ≥2 nitro groups are present (2,4-dinitrophenol pKa ~4.1; picric acid pKa ~0.7) does phenol become acidic enough to react with NaHCO₃.
edges.exception_trap: Adding FeBr₃ to phenol/aniline halogenation

edges.conditions: Conc H₂SO₄; Δ. Sulfonation of branched alkanes at tertiary C-H positions: (CH₃)₃CH + H₂SO₄ → (CH₃)₃C-SO₃H (tert-butylsulfonic acid). Not applicable to straight-chain alkanes. Reed reaction (SO₂Cl₂/hv) gives alkanesulfonyl chloride from straight-chain alkanes.
edges.notes: Corrected: to field was wrongly 'substituted_benzene'. Product of alkane + conc H2SO4 is a sulfonate (alkanesulfonic acid), not a substituted benzene. Reaction limited to branched alkanes at tertiary positions. Not a primary JEE transformation.

edges.conditions: α-Elimination of carbanion intermediate → carbene. Example: CHCl3 + t-BuOK → CHCl2⁻ (carbanion) → :CCl2 (dichlorocarbene) + Cl⁻. Dichlorocarbene reacts with alkenes → gem-dichlorocyclopropane (Reimer-Tiemann also proceeds via carbene). Strong base required (t-BuOK preferred over NaOH for carbene generation).
edges.notes: This represents the α-elimination step: carbanion (CHCl2⁻) → carbene (:CCl2) + Cl⁻. Mechanistically correct. The overall sequence is CHCl3 + strong base → carbanion intermediate → carbene. Not a general carbanion reaction — specific to haloform substrates.
edges.exception_description: Reimer_Tiemann_CHCl3_is_NOT_the_electrophile → In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, CHCl₃ is NOT the electrophile; the actual electrophile is dichlorocarbene (:CCl₂), generated from CHCl₃ by base (NaOH removes HCl: CHCl₃ + OH⁻ → :CCl₂ + Cl⁻ + H₂O). | RTR_one_ortho_blocked_gives_para_not_ortho → When both ortho positions of phenol are blocked (e.g., 2,6-di-substituted phenol), Reimer-Tiemann reaction gives the para product; if only one ortho is blocked, the free ortho is still preferred over para. | Reimer_Tiemann_gives_para_hydroxybenzaldehyde_as_major_product → Exception to the standard rule: Reimer tiemann gives para hydroxybenzaldehyde as major product. This is an exception case where the normal rule does not apply.
